usfs:ak-tongass-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest touches Glacier Bay National Park directly and lies about a mile from Wrangell–St. Elias, the country's largest national park at 13.1 million acres, so a Tongass trip can open onto some of the wildest protected land in North America. An international border with Canada follows the crest of the Boundary Ranges, tracing the divide between forest and country.
usfs:ak-tongass-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the mildest, driest window: June typically sees highs near 61°F and about 15 wet days, while July and August reach the mid-60s with 16 wet days each. Expect rain year-round — September through January average roughly 20 to 24 wet days per month.
usfs:ak-tongass-national-forest.whereToBase: Every gateway here sits inside the forest itself. Sitka, on the outer coast, is a well-known base, while Craig and Hoonah offer more remote footholds. Smaller communities such as Angoon and Tenakee Springs round out the options for reaching this island-and-fjord landscape.

usfs:al-conecuh-national-forest.whyCome: Conecuh National Forest spreads across southern Alabama along the Alabama–Florida line, in Covington and Escambia counties. The landscape is gentle here: level to moderately sloping terrain, broad ridges laced with stream terraces, and wide floodplains. It's a quiet slice of the coastal plain, well suited to travelers drawn to unhurried woods rather than dramatic elevation.
usfs:al-conecuh-national-forest.whenToGo: October typically brings the driest stretch, with highs near 79°F and only about 5 wet days. Spring is pleasant too: April usually sees highs around 78°F with roughly 9 wet days. Midsummer runs hot and humid, with July highs near 91°F and around 21 wet days.

usfs:al-talladega-national-forest.whyCome: At the southern edge of the Appalachian Mountains, Talladega National Forest spreads across some 392,567 acres of Alabama woodland. It's a place where the mountains taper into rolling ridges and forested hills, offering a quieter, less-trafficked corner of the Southeast for travelers drawn to hardwood forest and open country.
usfs:al-talladega-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest anchors a landscape of large man-made lakes built along Alabama's rivers. Martin Lake, the biggest at 162.6 square kilometers, was impounded by Martin Dam on the Tallapoosa River in 1926 and holds a campground and facilities. Lewis Smith Lake (79.6 sq km) and Logan Martin Lake (67.5 sq km) add further shoreline, all managed by Alabama Power Company.
usfs:al-talladega-national-forest.whenToGo: Autumn is typically the driest stretch: October sees highs near 76°F, lows around 56°F, and just 5 wet days. April is another comfortable window, typically around 74°F by day and 53°F at night with about 9 wet days. Midsummer runs hot and humid, with July highs near 90°F and 17 wet days.

usfs:al-tuskegee-national-forest.whyCome: Set in Macon County, Alabama, just north of Tuskegee and west of Auburn, this 15,649-acre national forest offers a gentle landscape of level to moderately sloping terrain. Broad ridges rise above stream terraces and wide floodplains, giving the forest an approachable, easygoing character for those seeking quiet woodland in the heart of the state.
usfs:al-tuskegee-national-forest.whenToGo: October is typically the driest stretch, with highs near 77°F, lows around 57°F, and only about 5 wet days. Spring brings milder warmth, with April typically reaching 76°F and around 8 wet days. Summer runs hot and humid, with July highs near 91°F and roughly 18 wet days.

usfs:al-william-b-bankhead-national-fore.whyCome: One of Alabama's four national forests, William B. Bankhead spreads across northwestern Alabama near the town of Double Springs. It safeguards the Sipsey Fork, Alabama's only National Wild and Scenic River, within a wooded landscape named for longtime U.S. representative William B. Bankhead. It is a quiet corner of the state built for those seeking rivers, forest, and space.
usfs:al-william-b-bankhead-national-fore.dontMiss: The forest's flagship water is Lewis Smith Lake, a man-made reservoir covering 79.6 square kilometers. It was formed by Lewis Smith Dam, completed in 1961 on the Sipsey Fork of the Warrior River and owned by Alabama Power Company. Paired with the Wild and Scenic Sipsey Fork, it anchors the region's paddling, fishing, and shoreline exploration.
usfs:al-william-b-bankhead-national-fore.whenToGo: October is typically the driest stretch, with highs near 74°F, lows around 54°F, and only about 6 wet days. Spring offers milder warmth, with April highs near 72°F and roughly 10 wet days. Midsummer runs hot and humid, with July highs near 89°F and about 17 wet days.

usfs:ar-ouachita-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning more than 2.7 million acres across western Arkansas and extreme-eastern Oklahoma, the Ouachita National Forest is a vast congressionally-designated forest of ridges, rivers, and reservoirs. Its scale makes it one of the region's great expanses of public land, offering room to roam far from the crowds and a genuine sense of wilderness.
usfs:ar-ouachita-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits just a mile from Hot Springs National Park and near the 1.5-million-acre Ozark-St. Francis National Forests, linking a broad web of public land. Anchoring the water is Lake Ouachita at 154.6 square kilometers, formed by Blakely Mountain Dam on the Ouachita River in 1953. De Gray Lake adds 11 campgrounds and facilities, with 10 more along Lake Greeson.
usfs:ar-ouachita-national-forest.whenToGo: For milder days, aim for autumn: October typically brings highs near 75°F, lows around 56°F, and just 8 wet days. Spring is greener but wetter, with April highs near 70°F and 11 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs around 91°F.
usfs:ar-ouachita-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit inside the forest itself. Mount Ida, Oden, and Norman all lie right at the heart of the woods, zero miles from the boundary, making them convenient jumping-off points for the lakes and ridges. Page and Black Springs offer additional interior footholds.

usfs:ar-ozark-st-francis-national-forest.whyCome: Sprawling across roughly 1,532,900 acres of Arkansas, the Ozark-St. Francis National Forests span forested ridges, rivers, and reservoirs. This is big, varied backcountry with room to roam, threaded by lakes and rolling uplands. It's a place for travelers who want space and water more than crowds, with landscapes that reward slow exploration across a full year of seasons.
usfs:ar-ozark-st-francis-national-forest.dontMiss: The forests hold several sizable man-made lakes worth planning around. Bull Shoals Lake covers about 182 square kilometers behind Bull Shoals Dam on the White River, with two campgrounds and facilities. Greers Ferry Lake adds five campgrounds and facilities. Nearby, the Ouachita National Forest lies just four miles away, extending the region's public land well past 2.7 million acres.
usfs:ar-ozark-st-francis-national-forest.whenToGo: Autumn is often the driest stretch: September typically sees highs near 84°F with only about 6 wet days, and October around 75°F with 7. Spring brings milder warmth—April highs near 70°F—though it runs wetter, with roughly 13 wet days in both April and May.
usfs:ar-ozark-st-francis-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small communities sit inside the forest boundary. Jasper lies within the forest, as do Scottsville, Nogo, Hector, and Freeman—all on forest land itself. Any makes a quiet starting point set among the ridges and rivers, keeping you close to trailheads and shoreline.

usfs:az-apache-sitgreaves-national-fores.whyCome: Spanning 2.76 million acres along the Mogollon Rim and the White Mountains in east-central Arizona and into New Mexico, this forest was formed from two once-separate units now managed as one. Home to more than 400 species of wildlife, its high elevation and cool summer breezes make it a popular weekend escape from the desert heat for Phoenix residents.
usfs:az-apache-sitgreaves-national-fores.dontMiss: The forest borders three others — Coconino, Gila, and Tonto National Forests — knitting it into a vast expanse of connected public land. Anglers and campers gravitate to Big Lake, whose 23 campgrounds and facilities anchor the most developed recreation here, and to Theodore Roosevelt Lake, held by the 1911 Bureau of Reclamation dam on the Salt River, with six campgrounds and a recreation area.
usfs:az-apache-sitgreaves-national-fores.whenToGo: Late spring is comfortable and dry: May typically sees highs near 76°F, lows around 44°F, and just 2 wet days. Autumn brings cooler air, with October highs around 70°F and lows near 42°F. Note the summer monsoon, when July and August average 9 to 10 wet days.
usfs:az-apache-sitgreaves-national-fores.whereToBase: Pinetop-Lakeside and Show Low both sit inside the forest, making them convenient bases. Just outside, Springerville lies 1.1 miles from the boundary, with Eagar close behind at 1.2 miles — both handy for reaching the eastern ranger districts and the Springerville Forest Supervisors Office.

usfs:az-coconino-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across roughly 2 million acres of northern Arizona near Flagstaff, the Coconino National Forest gathers an unusually varied landscape: deserts, ponderosa pine forests, mesas, alpine tundra, and ancient volcanic peaks. Elevations climb from 2,600 feet to Arizona's highest point at 12,633 feet. Designated a national forest in 1908, it remains one of the state's defining wild places.
usfs:az-coconino-national-forest.dontMiss: Coconino sits amid a near-continuous belt of public land, sharing boundaries with the Kaibab, Prescott, Tonto, and Apache-Sitgreaves National Forests, so the forested country extends well past its edges. For water, Mormon Lake is the forest's largest at 19.8 square kilometers, while Upper Lake Mary, held by a 1941 city of Flagstaff dam on Walnut Creek, offers four campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:az-coconino-national-forest.whenToGo: For mild, dry conditions, aim for late spring: May typically sees highs near 66°F, lows around 44°F, and about five wet days. Autumn is another reliable window, with October highs near 61°F and five wet days. Note that July and August turn wet, with 14 to 15 wet days each.
usfs:az-coconino-national-forest.whereToBase: Flagstaff, Sedona, and Cottonwood all sit inside the forest itself, putting you among the pines and red rock with no drive to the boundary. Just outside, Camp Verde lies 0.2 miles from the edge and Clarkdale about 2.4 miles, both handy for the forest's lower, warmer country.

usfs:az-coronado-national-forest.whyCome: Coronado National Forest sprawls across roughly 1.78 million acres of mountain ranges scattered through southeastern Arizona and southwestern New Mexico. Rather than a single block of land, it's an archipelago of forested peaks rising from desert basins, offering a landscape of range after range to explore across two states.
usfs:az-coronado-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a boundary with Saguaro National Park, so the two can be paired without a long drive between them. Apache-Sitgreaves National Forests lie about 24 miles away, extending the region's public land considerably. Within Coronado, the Tailings Water Reclamation Reservoir covers about 2.2 square kilometers and has two campgrounds and facilities nearby.
usfs:az-coronado-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is typically the driest stretch: April and May see highs of 75 to 83°F with just one wet day each. Winter runs mild, with January highs near 55°F. Avoid mid-summer if you dislike rain — July and August typically bring 9 to 10 wet days each.
usfs:az-coronado-national-forest.whereToBase: Safford sits just outside the forest, about 0.7 miles from the boundary. Patagonia lies roughly a mile out, and Maria Santisima del Carmen about 1.3 miles away. South Tucson, around 2.1 miles from the edge, offers a larger-town base within easy reach.

usfs:az-kaibab-national-forest.whyCome: Kaibab National Forest wraps both the north and south rims of the Grand Canyon in north-central Arizona, spanning 1.6 million acres across three districts. Elevations climb from 5,500 feet in the southwest corner to 10,418 feet at the summit of Kendrick Peak. Headquartered in Williams, it offers a high-country counterpoint to the canyon it surrounds.
usfs:az-kaibab-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders Grand Canyon National Park directly, with the park's 1.2 million acres separating the North and South Kaibab districts. It also shares boundaries with the Coconino and Prescott National Forests and sits about 24 miles from Zion National Park, placing several of the Southwest's landmark landscapes within easy reach.
usfs:az-kaibab-national-forest.whenToGo: Late spring is typically comfortable, with April highs near 62°F and just three wet days. September is another mild window, with highs around 77°F and about five wet days. August is historically the wettest, averaging roughly 12 wet days.
usfs:az-kaibab-national-forest.whereToBase: Tusayan and Williams both sit inside the forest at the boundary. Fredonia lies about 1.4 miles out, Kanab roughly 5.9 miles, and Flagstaff around 11 miles from the boundary.

usfs:az-prescott-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 1.25 million acres in north central Arizona, Prescott National Forest rises in the mountains southwest of Flagstaff and north of Phoenix, near the town of Prescott. Set largely in Yavapai County, with a small reach into Coconino County, it offers a broad canvas of high-country terrain within easy range of Arizona's two major cities.
usfs:az-prescott-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits shoulder to shoulder with three neighbors, opening onto a vast web of connected public land: Coconino National Forest, Kaibab National Forest, and the 2.9 million-acre Tonto National Forest. Within reach is Horseshoe Reservoir, a 7.9-square-kilometer man-made lake on the Verde River behind Horseshoe Dam (1946, Bureau of Reclamation), with two campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:az-prescott-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is comfortable and dry: April typically sees highs near 69°F, lows around 42°F, and just 2 wet days, with similar conditions in May. Fall offers another mild window, October averaging a 72°F high, a 48°F low, and about 3 wet days.
usfs:az-prescott-national-forest.whereToBase: Camp Verde and Jerome both sit inside the forest at its boundary. Clarkdale lies just 0.9 miles out, Chino Valley about 2 miles, and Cottonwood roughly 2.9 miles from the boundary, giving several nearby options for approaching the forest.

usfs:az-tonto-national-forest.whyCome: The largest of Arizona's six national forests and the ninth largest in the country, Tonto spans 2,873,200 acres of remarkably diverse terrain. Elevations climb from 1,400 feet in the Sonoran Desert to 7,400 feet among the ponderosa pines of the Mogollon Rim. It is also one of the most visited urban forests in the United States.
usfs:az-tonto-national-forest.dontMiss: Tonto shares unbroken boundaries with three other national forests — Apache-Sitgreaves, Coconino, and Prescott — extending the wild country in every direction. Its reservoirs invite exploration: Theodore Roosevelt Lake, held by a 1911 dam on the Salt River, offers six campgrounds and facilities plus a recreation area, while Bartlett and Horseshoe on the Verde River each add two more.
usfs:az-tonto-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is the sweet spot: April typically brings highs near 80°F, lows around 51°F, and just 2 wet days. Autumn follows suit, with October highs around 82°F, lows near 56°F, and roughly 3 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs typically reaching 101°F.
usfs:az-tonto-national-forest.whereToBase: Payson, Superior, and Star Valley all sit inside the forest, placing you right among the terrain. Just outside, Globe lies 0.6 miles from the boundary and Miami about 1.1 miles, both handy jumping-off points for the eastern reaches.

usfs:ca-angeles-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 706,719 acres in the San Gabriel and Sierra Pelona Mountains north of Los Angeles, Angeles National Forest offers a rugged escape at the edge of the city. Managed by the US Forest Service, it also oversees a majority of the San Gabriel Mountains National Monument, giving Southern Californians high country within easy reach.
usfs:ca-angeles-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits shoulder to shoulder with public land: it directly borders Los Padres National Forest (1,970,266 acres) and San Bernardino National Forest (805,483 acres), with Cleveland National Forest about 18 miles away. Three man-made lakes anchor recreation, including Pyramid Lake, fed by the 1974 Pyramid Dam, with 11 campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:ca-angeles-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is comfortable, with April typically reaching highs of 69°F and lows of 48°F over about 3 wet days, and May climbing to 75°F. Autumn brings similar ease: October typically sees highs near 77°F, lows of 57°F, and just 2 wet days.
usfs:ca-angeles-national-forest.whereToBase: The forest is ringed by close-in towns. Duarte sits just 0.3 miles from the boundary, with Glendora at 0.4 miles. To the west, La Cañada Flintridge and Sierra Madre both lie about 0.7 miles out, offering flexible jumping-off points along different flanks of the mountains.

usfs:ca-cleveland-national-forest.whyCome: Cleveland National Forest is the southernmost national forest in California, spreading across 561,887 acres of inland montane country in San Diego, Riverside, and Orange counties. Roughly 60 miles from the Pacific, it gathers chaparral canyons, arroyos, high desert, oak and conifer groves, and meadows. Near its water sources, riparian pockets draw native and migratory wildlife, notably at man-made Lake Cuyamaca.
usfs:ca-cleveland-national-forest.dontMiss: Water is a quiet highlight here. Man-made Diamond Valley Lake is the largest at 19.9 square kilometers, held by its namesake dam completed in 2000. Natural Lake Elsinore covers 12.8 square kilometers with two campgrounds and facilities, while historic Sweetwater Reservoir traces back to an 1888 dam. Lake Henshaw and Perris Reservoir round out the region's reservoirs and recreation areas.
usfs:ca-cleveland-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is typically mild, with April highs near 69°F, lows around 49°F, and about five wet days. For warmer, drier conditions, summer delivers: July typically sees highs of 85°F, lows near 63°F, and essentially no rain. Cooler months can bring morning frost and snowfall.
usfs:ca-cleveland-national-forest.whereToBase: All bases sit just outside the forest. Pauma is closest at about 1.1 miles from the boundary, followed by Cuca at 1.4 miles and Guejito at 1.5 miles. San Jose Del Valle (2 miles) and Cuyamaca (2.1 miles) offer further nearby footholds along the forest's edge.

usfs:ca-eldorado-national-forest.whyCome: Eldorado National Forest spans 793,653 acres of the central Sierra Nevada in eastern California, a landscape of high-country lakes, granite ridges, and mountain waters. Its position among some of the West's largest forested lands makes it a gateway to the range's classic terrain, drawing travelers toward the reservoirs and peaks that define this stretch of the Sierra.
usfs:ca-eldorado-national-forest.dontMiss: Eldorado shares unbroken boundaries with three sprawling national forests—Stanislaus, Tahoe, and the vast Humboldt-Toiyabe—stitching it into a nearly continuous swath of public land. For water-based recreation, Union Valley Reservoir counts 16 campgrounds and facilities, while French Meadows and Fallen Leaf Lake each offer 13. Lake Tahoe, at 497.7 square kilometers, anchors the forest's northeastern edge.
usfs:ca-eldorado-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 86°F, lows around 57°F, and essentially no wet days, with August close behind. For milder conditions, June offers highs of 77°F and only three wet days, while September stays warm at 76°F.
usfs:ca-eldorado-national-forest.whereToBase: Placerville sits closest at 2.9 miles from the boundary, making it a practical staging point. South Lake Tahoe lies 7.6 miles out toward the lake country, and Colfax is 9.8 miles from the edge. Auburn (11.3 miles) offers another nearby option for reaching the forest.

usfs:ca-inyo-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning the eastern Sierra Nevada and the White Mountains, Inyo National Forest holds a remarkable set of superlatives: Mount Whitney, the highest point in the contiguous United States; Boundary Peak, the highest point in Nevada; and the Ancient Bristlecone Pine Forest, home to the oldest living trees in the world. Encompassing much of the Owens Valley, it remains one of the least wooded forests in the system.
usfs:ca-inyo-national-forest.dontMiss: This is deep public-land country. Inyo contains part of Death Valley National Park and shares boundaries with Yosemite, Kings Canyon, and Sequoia National Parks, along with the vast Humboldt-Toiyabe, Sierra, and Sequoia National Forests, so a single trip can string together several protected landscapes. Nearby waters include natural Mono Lake, at 182.6 square kilometers, and Owens Lake, at 69.9 square kilometers.
usfs:ca-inyo-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the high-elevation window: July typically sees highs near 57 and lows around 40, with about 8 wet days, easing to a drier 6 in August. Early fall is a quieter option, with October highs near 40, lows around 23, and typically just 3 wet days.
usfs:ca-inyo-national-forest.whereToBase: Mammoth Lakes sits inside the forest, making it the most immediate base. Bishop lies just outside, about 0.2 miles from the boundary. Farther afield, Big Tree is roughly 24.1 miles from the boundary for travelers approaching from another direction.

usfs:ca-klamath-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning roughly 1.7 million acres across the Klamath Mountains and Cascade Range in northern California, this forest was established in 1905. Continuous stands of ponderosa pine, Jeffrey pine, Douglas fir and incense cedar cover the terrain, with an estimated 168,000 acres of old-growth. Within its boundaries lie Kangaroo Lake and the historic Sawyers Bar Catholic Church.
usfs:ca-klamath-national-forest.dontMiss: The Klamath shares its boundary with several major forests, including Shasta-Trinity, Modoc, Six Rivers and Rogue River-Siskiyou, opening a vast stretch of connected public land. Redwood National Park sits about 17 miles away. Among nearby reservoirs, Trinity Lake covers 63.1 square kilometers behind Trinity Dam (1962), while Shasta Lake spans 50.2 square kilometers behind Shasta Dam (1945).
usfs:ca-klamath-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest window: July typically sees highs near 89°F, lows around 57°F and just 2 wet days, with August similar. For milder conditions, late spring into June brings highs from 65 to 77°F and 4 to 6 wet days. Winter runs wet, with December averaging 14 rainy days.
usfs:ca-klamath-national-forest.whereToBase: Etna sits just 0.2 miles from the boundary, with Fort Jones close behind at 0.4 miles. Yreka, home to the forest headquarters, lies about 0.5 miles out. Farther afield, Dorris is roughly 2.1 miles from the boundary and Montague about 5.5 miles.

usfs:ca-lassen-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across roughly 1,700 square miles of northeastern California, Lassen National Forest carries the name of Peter Lassen, the pioneer who mined, ranched and guided emigrant parties through the region in the 1850s. Today its volcanic country, high lakes and forested ridges reward travelers looking for a quieter corner of the state.
usfs:ca-lassen-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken boundary with Lassen Volcanic National Park and neighbors several vast forests, including Shasta-Trinity and Modoc. Water defines much of the recreation: Lake Almanor covers about 100 square kilometers behind a 1927 PG&E dam and offers seven campgrounds and facilities, while natural Eagle Lake anchors a named recreation area with its own campgrounds.
usfs:ca-lassen-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 89°F, lows around 56°F and just one wet day, with August close behind. For milder conditions, September and October typically bring highs in the 65–77°F range and only five to six wet days.
usfs:ca-lassen-national-forest.whereToBase: Susanville sits closest, about 0.2 miles from the boundary, making it a natural gateway. Anderson (4.6 miles) and Redding (4.8 miles) lie a short distance out on the forest's western side, offering additional starting points for exploring the region.

usfs:ca-los-padres-national-forest.whyCome: Los Padres National Forest spans nearly 2 million acres of mountainous country along the California coast, stretching from Ventura to Monterey and reaching inland. Elevations climb from sea level to 8,847 feet, folding the Pacific shoreline into rugged high ground. Administered by the U.S. Forest Service, it offers a broad sweep of central and southern California landscape.
usfs:ca-los-padres-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a boundary with Angeles National Forest and sits near Channel Islands and Pinnacles National Parks, both about 13 miles away, so a wider public-land itinerary is easy to assemble. Man-made lakes anchor the water recreation: Lake Cachuma, the largest at 10.5 square kilometers, backs up behind Bradbury Dam on the Santa Ynez River, completed in 1953.
usfs:ca-los-padres-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is comfortable and dry: April typically sees highs near 69°F with only about 2 wet days, and May around 76°F with roughly 1. Autumn holds steady, with October highs near 76°F and about 1 wet day. Winter runs cooler and wetter, with December highs near 57°F and around 7 wet days.
usfs:ca-los-padres-national-forest.whereToBase: Navajo sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Just outside, Arroyo Grande lies about 0.2 miles from the boundary and Santa Margarita about 0.9 miles, both convenient staging points for exploring the surrounding country.

usfs:ca-mendocino-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than a million acres in California's Coastal Mountain Range, Mendocino is the only national forest in the state with no major paved road entering it. That isolation is the point: camping, hiking, mountain biking, backpacking, boating, fishing, hunting, paragliding, and nature study unfold across country that stays quiet and undeveloped.
usfs:ca-mendocino-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares boundaries with the vast Shasta-Trinity and Six Rivers National Forests, opening a nearly continuous stretch of public land to explore. For water, Black Butte Lake, formed by Black Butte Dam on Stony Creek in 1963, is a named recreation area with two campgrounds. Nearby Clear Lake, a natural lake, covers 160.5 square kilometers.
usfs:ca-mendocino-national-forest.whenToGo: Late spring is typically pleasant and dry: May sees highs near 82°F with only about 2 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs around 101°F and virtually no rain. April, with highs near 71°F and roughly 4 wet days, offers a cooler window before the heat arrives.
usfs:ca-mendocino-national-forest.whereToBase: Red Bluff sits closest, about 1.1 miles from the boundary, with La Barranca Colorada around 3.4 miles out. Chico lies roughly 3.5 miles from the forest edge. All three are outside the boundary and make practical launch points for trips into the backcountry.

usfs:ca-modoc-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across roughly 1,654,392 acres in northeastern California, Modoc National Forest is a vast, quiet expanse of high country far from the crowds. This is remote California — big skies, natural lakes, and open forest where solitude comes easily. For travelers drawn to space and stillness rather than headline attractions, Modoc rewards the effort of getting here.
usfs:ca-modoc-national-forest.dontMiss: Modoc sits within an enormous corridor of connected public land, sharing boundaries with the Klamath, Lassen, Shasta-Trinity, and Fremont-Winema National Forests, with Lassen Volcanic National Park about 24 miles away — meaning near-endless room to roam. Water is a defining feature: natural Goose Lake spans about 377 square kilometers, and Middle Alkali Lake covers roughly 150.
usfs:ca-modoc-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer brings the warmest, driest conditions: July typically sees highs near 91°F, lows around 62°F, and just one wet day. September stays pleasant, with highs near 77°F and about five wet days. Winters are cold and damp, with December highs around 42°F.
usfs:ca-modoc-national-forest.whereToBase: Alturas sits closest, just 0.9 miles from the boundary. Malin lies about 7 miles out and Tulelake roughly 7.4 miles away, both making practical entry points from the north side of the forest.

usfs:ca-plumas-national-forest.whyCome: Plumas National Forest spreads across roughly 1,146,000 acres at the northern terminus of the Sierra Nevada in northern California. It takes its name from its primary watershed, the Rio de las Plumas, or Feather River. Part of the forest is old-growth, recognized by the Old-Growth Forest Network — a landscape of mountain water and mature timber for those drawn to quieter high country.
usfs:ca-plumas-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares direct boundaries with Lassen and Tahoe National Forests, opening onto a vast stretch of connected public land. Its lakes reward a visit: man-made Lake Almanor, backed by a 1927 PG&E dam, spans about 100 square kilometers with seven campgrounds and facilities, while New Bullards Bar Reservoir on the North Yuba River adds eight more.
usfs:ca-plumas-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs around 85°F, lows near 54°F, and just one wet day. For milder conditions, June and September bring highs in the 70s with only four or five wet days. Winter months like December average highs around 42°F with roughly 13 wet days.
usfs:ca-plumas-national-forest.whereToBase: Portola sits closest, about 0.3 miles from the boundary. Paradise lies roughly 3.9 miles out, and Susanville about 7.7 miles away, all just beyond the forest edge and convenient for day trips into the surrounding country.

usfs:ca-san-bernardino-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning 805,483 acres across Southern California, San Bernardino National Forest gathers the eastern San Gabriel Mountains, the San Bernardino Mountains, and the San Jacinto and Santa Rosa ranges. Elevations climb from 2,000 to 11,499 feet, and the forest holds seven wilderness areas, among them San Gorgonio, Cucamonga, San Jacinto and Santa Rosa. The Juan Bautista de Anza National Historic Trail crosses part of it.
usfs:ca-san-bernardino-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a boundary with Angeles National Forest, with Cleveland National Forest about 8 miles off and Joshua Tree National Park roughly 12 miles away, opening a wide sweep of adjacent public land. For water, Big Bear Lake anchors the recreation with 18 campgrounds and facilities, while natural Lake Elsinore and Baldwin Lake add more shoreline options.
usfs:ca-san-bernardino-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring and fall bring the mildest conditions. April and May typically see highs of 69 to 76°F with around 7 wet days each. October is usually warmer, near 78°F with about 4 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs around 94°F but nearly no rain.
usfs:ca-san-bernardino-national-forest.whereToBase: Big Bear Lake sits inside the forest itself, right on the boundary. San Bernardino lies about 1 mile out, with Banning around 1.2 miles and Highland roughly 1.5 miles from the boundary, all offering nearby footholds for exploring the mountains.

usfs:ca-sequoia-national-forest.whyCome: Named for the giant sequoias that populate 38 distinct groves within its boundaries, this southern Sierra Nevada forest spreads across roughly 1,160,000 acres of California mountain country. The majestic trees are the headline draw, but the surrounding high terrain of ridges, rivers, and reservoirs rounds out a landscape built for slow, unhurried exploration.
usfs:ca-sequoia-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders two national parks, Kings Canyon and Sequoia, plus the neighboring Sierra and Inyo National Forests, so the protected country extends well beyond its own million-plus acres. For water time, Isabella Lake, held by Isabella Dam on the Kern River since 1953, anchors 19 campgrounds and facilities, while Pine Flat Lake on the Kings River adds a named recreation area.
usfs:ca-sequoia-national-forest.whenToGo: For warm, dry days, aim for summer: July typically sees highs around 84°F with just one wet day, and June and August stay in the high 70s to low 80s with only three. Spring is milder but wetter, with March averaging about 12 wet days.
usfs:ca-sequoia-national-forest.whereToBase: All the handiest jumping-off points sit outside the boundary. El Tejon is closest at about 10.6 miles out, followed by Porterville around 13.4 miles and Orange Cove near 14.3 miles. Larger Bakersfield lies roughly 15.2 miles from the edge if you want more services before heading up.

usfs:ca-shasta-trinity-national-forest.whyCome: The largest National Forest in California spreads across 2,715,500 acres of northern mountains, five wilderness areas, hundreds of alpine lakes, and 6,278 miles of streams and rivers. Its defining landmarks are Mount Shasta, rising 14,179 feet, and Shasta Lake, the largest man-made lake in the state — a landscape built for water, high country, and long horizons.
usfs:ca-shasta-trinity-national-forest.dontMiss: Trinity Lake, the largest here at 63.1 square kilometers, was formed by Trinity Dam on the Trinity River in 1962 and offers four campgrounds and facilities. Shasta Lake, held by 1945's Shasta Dam, adds five. The forest shares unbroken borders with five other National Forests — Klamath, Lassen, Mendocino, Modoc, and Six Rivers — extending public land in every direction, with Redwood National Park about 21 miles off.
usfs:ca-shasta-trinity-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer runs dry and warm: July typically reaches highs near 88°F with just one wet day, and August stays close behind at 85°F. For milder temperatures, late spring brings May highs around 67°F with only four wet days. Winters are cool and damp, with December highs of 42°F and 13 wet days.
usfs:ca-shasta-trinity-national-forest.whereToBase: Mount Shasta sits just 0.4 miles from the boundary, with Dunsmuir a comparable 0.5 miles out. Weed lies 1.3 miles away, while the town of Shasta Lake is 2.1 miles from the edge and Redding about 9.6 miles beyond, giving a range of nearby options across the region.

usfs:ca-sierra-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 1,418,790 acres on the western slope of California's central Sierra Nevada, Sierra National Forest is known for its mountain scenery and beautiful lakes. It sits bounded on the northwest by Yosemite National Park and on the south by Kings Canyon National Park, placing high-country peaks, forested ridges, and reservoirs within a single expanse.
usfs:ca-sierra-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders Kings Canyon National Park and adjoins Sequoia, Stanislaus, and Inyo National Forests, threading together a vast public-land corridor. Its lakes anchor the recreation: Pine Flat Lake, at 19.7 square kilometers behind the 1954 Pine Flat Dam on the Kings River, is the largest, with a named recreation area. Shaver Lake and Lake Thomas A. Edison add campgrounds and further shoreline access.
usfs:ca-sierra-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 82°F, lows around 57°F, and essentially no wet days, while August runs to 80°F with just two. For cooler conditions, October typically brings highs of 63°F and only two wet days, though nights drop toward 41°F.
usfs:ca-sierra-national-forest.whereToBase: Big Tree sits closest, about 1.1 miles from the boundary, followed by Mammoth Lakes at 6.3 miles and Las Mariposas at 9.4 miles. All lie just outside the forest, offering staging points for trips into the high country and lake basins.

usfs:ca-six-rivers-national-forest.whyCome: Six Rivers National Forest sprawls across more than 1.27 million acres in the northwestern corner of California. This is remote, water-laced country in the far reaches of the state, where forest gives way to lakes and river valleys. It rewards travelers willing to venture beyond the coast into California's quieter, wilder edge.
usfs:ca-six-rivers-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits amid a vast web of public land, sharing boundaries with Redwood National Park and four national forests, including the 2.7-million-acre Shasta-Trinity, so exploration extends well past its borders. For water, Ruth Lake spans 4.3 square kilometers with two campgrounds, while man-made Applegate Lake, held by the 1980 Applegate Dam, offers eight campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:ca-six-rivers-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 91°F with essentially no wet days, and August runs about 90°F with just one. For milder conditions, late spring brings May highs around 72°F with roughly six wet days. Winter is cool and damp, with December averaging 17 wet days.
usfs:ca-six-rivers-national-forest.whereToBase: All bases sit outside the forest. Trinidad is closest at about 5.1 miles from the boundary, followed by Crescent City near the coast at 7.3 miles. Arcata, roughly 9.1 miles out, rounds out a trio of northern California coastal towns for stocking up before heading inland.

usfs:ca-stanislaus-national-forest.whyCome: One of America's oldest national forests, Stanislaus was established on February 22, 1897 and manages 898,099 acres across four counties in the Sierra Nevada of Northern California. Named after the Stanislaus River, it stretches across more than a million acres of mountain country, offering the classic high-country landscape of the range.
usfs:ca-stanislaus-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest wraps around Yosemite National Park and shares boundaries with the Sierra, Eldorado, and vast Humboldt-Toiyabe national forests, opening onto a nearly continuous run of public land. Its reservoirs anchor the water recreation: Don Pedro, the largest at 44.4 square kilometers, held back by a 1971 dam, and New Melones Lake on the Stanislaus River, with three campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:ca-stanislaus-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer typically brings the warmest, driest stretch: July highs near 82°F with only about 2 wet days, and August around 79°F. For milder conditions, September and October typically see highs in the 60s and 70s with roughly 5 wet days per month.
usfs:ca-stanislaus-national-forest.whereToBase: Sonora sits closest, about 0.4 miles from the boundary. Angels lies roughly 8.4 miles out, and Las Mariposas about 9.6 miles from the forest edge — all just outside for an easy approach from the west.

usfs:ca-tahoe-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across parts of six California counties northwest of Lake Tahoe, Tahoe National Forest gathers more than a million acres of Sierra Nevada high country. Its centerpiece is the 8,587-foot Sierra Buttes near Sierra City, a summit that on clear days reaches all the way to Mount Lassen and Mount Shasta on the horizon.
usfs:ca-tahoe-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits shoulder-to-shoulder with Plumas, Eldorado, and the vast Humboldt-Toiyabe National Forests, so a trip here can stretch across an unbroken sweep of public land. Union Valley Reservoir, dammed on Big Silver Creek in 1962, anchors sixteen campgrounds and facilities, while New Bullards Bar Reservoir on the North Yuba adds eight more for lakeside stays.
usfs:ca-tahoe-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 78°F with just 2 wet days, and August runs around 75°F with 3. Fall shoulder days in September stay pleasant near 68°F. Winters are cold and damp, with December highs near 39°F and about 14 wet days.
usfs:ca-tahoe-national-forest.whereToBase: Truckee sits inside the forest itself, making it the most convenient base. Loyalton lies just 0.8 miles beyond the boundary, while Grass Valley and Nevada City are roughly 2 and 2.8 miles out, offering approachable jumping-off points to the west.

usfs:co-arapaho-and-roosevelt-national-f.whyCome: Spanning nearly 2.5 million acres across Colorado's high country, Arapaho and Roosevelt National Forests offer a vast expanse of Rocky Mountain terrain. Reservoirs, alpine ridges, and forested valleys stretch in every direction, all within reach of the Front Range. This is landscape built for those who want mountains at scale, close to home.
usfs:co-arapaho-and-roosevelt-national-f.dontMiss: The forest shares borders with Rocky Mountain National Park and four neighboring national forests, including the 4.5-million-acre Medicine Bow-Routt, creating an almost unbroken run of public land. Water defines the recreation: Lake Granby, the largest at 28.6 square kilometers, sits behind Granby Dam on the Colorado River and anchors a named recreation area with eight campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:co-arapaho-and-roosevelt-national-f.whenToGo: Summer brings the warmest, driest window: July typically sees highs near 90°F, lows around 61°F, and only 6 wet days. For crisp shoulder-season conditions, October averages a 68°F high, 40°F low, and just 3 wet days per month—among the driest of the year.
usfs:co-arapaho-and-roosevelt-national-f.whereToBase: Nederland sits inside the forest with 4 lodging options, 13 places to eat, and a local brewery. Grand Lake, just 0.3 miles from the boundary, offers 6 lodgings and 16 dining spots. Winter Park lies within the forest as well, a convenient interior base.

usfs:co-grand-mesa-uncompahgre-and-gunni.whyCome: Spanning more than 3.1 million acres across western Colorado, this vast forest joins three named landscapes—Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison—into a single high-country expanse of mountains, reservoirs and alpine terrain. It's a place shaped by scale, with room for scenic drives, water recreation and access to some of the state's most storied peaks and canyons.
usfs:co-grand-mesa-uncompahgre-and-gunni.dontMiss: The forest shares its borders with a ring of public land, including the San Juan, White River, Rio Grande, and Pike and San Isabel National Forests, and sits just four miles from Black Canyon of the Gunnison National Park—stitching together a huge continuous wilderness. Among its reservoirs, Blue Mesa is the largest at 36.2 square kilometers, held by the Bureau of Reclamation's Blue Mesa Dam on the Gunnison River (1966), with six campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:co-grand-mesa-uncompahgre-and-gunni.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically peaks around a high of 83°F and a low of 54°F with about 9 wet days, while September eases to a high of 72°F, a low of 45°F, and roughly 7 wet days—the driest stretch of the year. Winters run cold, with January highs near 31°F.
usfs:co-grand-mesa-uncompahgre-and-gunni.whereToBase: Several towns sit inside the forest boundary. Ouray offers about 27 lodgings and 22 places to eat, known for hot springs and skiing. Telluride, also a ski and hot-springs town, has around 19 lodgings and 20 dining spots. Mountain Village and Mount Crested Butte provide further slopeside options.

usfs:co-pike-and-san-isabel-national-for.whyCome: Spread across nearly 3.97 million acres of Colorado high country, Pike and San Isabel National Forests offer a vast sweep of mountains, reservoirs, and canyons. The landscape ranges from the peaks and creeks of the Front Range to broad valleys threaded with man-made lakes, making it a sprawling playground for anyone drawn to the state's alpine terrain.
usfs:co-pike-and-san-isabel-national-for.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken boundary with Great Sand Dunes National Park and several neighboring forests, including the Rio Grande and White River, widening the range of public land at your doorstep. Among its waters, Dillon Reservoir stands out with 18 campgrounds and facilities, while natural Elevenmile Canyon Reservoir adds two more for quieter shorelines.
usfs:co-pike-and-san-isabel-national-for.whenToGo: Early fall is typically pleasant, with September highs near 83°F, lows around 58°F, and only about 4 wet days. October cools to highs near 70°F with just 2 wet days. Spring shifts wetter, May averaging 10 wet days despite comfortable 73°F highs.
usfs:co-pike-and-san-isabel-national-for.whereToBase: Green Mountain Falls and Alma both sit inside the forest at the boundary. Just outside, Fairplay lies 0.2 miles away, Woodland Park 0.4 miles, and Salida 0.5 miles, all offering easy access to the surrounding high country.

usfs:co-rio-grande-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning 1.86 million acres in southwestern Colorado, Rio Grande National Forest encompasses the San Luis Valley — the world's largest agricultural alpine valley — and one of the world's largest high deserts. The Rio Grande rises here, and the Continental Divide runs along most of the forest's western border, tracing terrain across parts of nine counties.
usfs:co-rio-grande-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken boundary with Great Sand Dunes National Park and four national forests, including the Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison and Pike and San Isabel forests — a vast contiguous stretch of public land. Vallecito Reservoir, held by the 1941 Vallecito Dam on the Los Pinos River, offers a named recreation area with six campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:co-rio-grande-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the mildest window: July and August typically see highs of 80°F and 77°F with lows in the high 40s, though both bring around nine wet days. Early fall is drier — September typically reaches 71°F with just five wet days, and October eases to 59°F with four.
usfs:co-rio-grande-national-forest.whereToBase: The City of Creede sits inside the forest itself. Just outside, Bonanza and Saguache each lie about 0.2 miles from the boundary, while South Fork is roughly 0.3 miles out and Del Norte, home to the forest headquarters, about 0.4 miles away.

usfs:co-routt-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 1,249,143 acres in northern Colorado, Routt National Forest is part of the larger Medicine Bow–Routt system, three areas administratively combined in 1995 for their shared resources and proximity. It's high-country terrain of mountains, reservoirs, and forest, sitting shoulder to shoulder with some of Colorado's most storied public lands.
usfs:co-routt-national-forest.dontMiss: Routt borders Rocky Mountain National Park and adjoins the Arapaho and Roosevelt and White River National Forests, opening a vast, connected stretch of high country. Its reservoirs anchor the recreation here: Lake Granby, the largest at 28.6 square kilometers, holds eight campgrounds and a named recreation area, its waters raised by the Bureau of Reclamation's Granby Dam on the Colorado River in 1950.
usfs:co-routt-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July and August typically bring highs of 69°F and 67°F with lows in the mid-40s, though expect around 13 wet days each month. September eases to a 60°F high with just 9 wet days. Winters are cold, with January highs near 19°F.

usfs:co-san-juan-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than 1,878,846 acres of western Colorado, San Juan National Forest covers most of the southern San Juan Mountains west of the Continental Divide. It holds two alpine wilderness areas, the Weminuche and South San Juan, along with the Piedra Area. The historic Durango and Silverton Narrow Gauge Railroad threads through the forest.
usfs:co-san-juan-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders the Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison National Forests and the Rio Grande National Forest, extending wild country in nearly every direction, with Mesa Verde National Park just 6 miles off. Water draws too: Navajo Reservoir spans 62.2 square kilometers behind its 1963 dam, while Vallecito Reservoir offers six campgrounds and a named recreation area.
usfs:co-san-juan-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the warm window: July typically reaches highs near 65°F with lows around 45°F, though it also brings roughly 18 wet days, and August climbs to about 20. For drier conditions, November typically sees only 6 wet days, with highs near 34°F and lows around 14°F.
usfs:co-san-juan-national-forest.whereToBase: Durango, half a mile from the boundary, has the most infrastructure, with 22 lodgings, 56 places to eat, plus ski and brewery scenes. Pagosa Springs, 0.2 miles out, offers 9 lodgings, 15 eateries and hot springs. Rico sits inside the forest itself.

usfs:co-white-river-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than 2.4 million acres of northwest Colorado, White River is the most visited National Forest in the United States, drawing much of its traffic from the twelve ski areas within its boundaries. Named for the White River that runs through its northern section, it pairs high-country recreation with the scale of Colorado's central mountains.
usfs:co-white-river-national-forest.dontMiss: White River sits amid an unbroken web of public land, sharing boundaries with the Grand Mesa, Uncompahgre and Gunnison, Medicine Bow-Routt, Arapaho and Roosevelt, Pike and San Isabel, and Routt National Forests. Its reservoirs anchor the recreation: Lake Granby, at 28.6 square kilometers behind Granby Dam, holds eight campgrounds and facilities, while Dillon Reservoir and Turquoise Lake each offer eighteen.
usfs:co-white-river-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest and warmest window: July typically sees highs near 86°F, lows around 58°F, and just 3 wet days, with August close behind at 84°F. Winter brings the ski season—January typically holds highs of 31°F and 9 wet days, so pack accordingly.
usfs:co-white-river-national-forest.whereToBase: Dillon and Silverthorne both sit inside the forest at the boundary; Dillon offers the most range with 10 lodgings and 24 places to eat, plus a brewery, while Silverthorne adds 5 lodgings, 4 eateries and its own brewery. Montezuma, also within the forest, makes a quieter high-country option.

usfs:fl-apalachicola-national-forest.whyCome: The largest national forest in Florida and the only one in the Panhandle, Apalachicola spreads across more than 633,000 acres of managed woodland and water. It's a landscape built for the outdoors: off-road biking, hiking, swimming, boating, hunting, fishing, horseback riding, and ATV riding all share the same sprawling terrain.
usfs:fl-apalachicola-national-forest.dontMiss: Water defines much of the forest. Lake Talquin, a man-made reservoir of roughly 33 square kilometers, was formed by the Jackson Bluff Dam on the Ochlockonee River, completed in 1928. Nearby, the natural Lake Wimico (about 16 square kilometers) and Lake Jackson (about 15 square kilometers) round out the region's boating and fishing options.
usfs:fl-apalachicola-national-forest.whenToGo: For the driest, mildest conditions, aim for autumn: October and November typically bring highs of 81 and 73, lows near 61 and 53, and just 6 and 5 wet days. Early spring is another window, with March highs around 75. Summer runs hot and wet, with July averaging 25 rainy days.

usfs:fl-ocala-national-forest.whyCome: The second largest nationally protected forest in Florida, Ocala spreads across 607 square miles of North Central Florida. Established in 1908, it is the oldest national forest east of the Mississippi River and the southernmost in the continental U.S. Its name traces to a Timucuan term meaning "fair land" or "big hammock," and it sits just three miles east of Ocala.
usfs:fl-ocala-national-forest.dontMiss: Water defines this forest. Lake George, natural and roughly 176 square kilometers, is the standout, with a campground and facilities on its shores. Beyond it lie a string of large natural lakes: Lake Harris at about 75 square kilometers, Crescent Lake near 69, Lake Griffin at 38, and Lake Monroe at 36 — a broad spread of open water for exploring.
usfs:fl-ocala-national-forest.whenToGo: For the driest, mildest conditions, aim for late fall through winter. November and December typically see highs of 75 and 71 with lows of 59 and 54, and only about six wet days each month. January is cooler, near 67 by day. Summer brings heat in the 90s and 20-plus wet days.

usfs:fl-osceola-national-forest.whyCome: Set in northeast Florida, Osceola National Forest spreads across 164,912 acres of pine flatwoods and cypress country. Named for the Seminole warrior Osceola and established by President Herbert Hoover's proclamation on July 10, 1931, the forest carries a long history of Native American presence, with people inhabiting and traveling through this land.
usfs:fl-osceola-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest's centerpiece is Ocean Pond, a natural lake covering 7.3 square kilometers. Its shores hold two campgrounds and facilities, making it a natural gathering point for camping and time on the water within the wider expanse of this 164,912-acre forest.
usfs:fl-osceola-national-forest.whenToGo: For the mildest, driest conditions, aim for late fall: November typically sees highs near 73°F, lows around 53°F, and just 5 wet days. Winter stays comfortable too, with January highs around 64°F. Summer runs hot and stormy, with July and August averaging 22 to 23 wet days.

usfs:ga-chattahoochee-oconee-national-fo.whyCome: Spanning roughly 1.8 million acres across northern Georgia, this forest joins two distinct landscapes: the Oconee in the east and the Chattahoochee in the North Georgia Mountains, whose western reaches take in Johns Mountain, Little Sand Mountain, and Taylor Ridge. Rabun County alone holds nearly 148,684 acres, part of a sprawling public-land expanse that anchors the region's mountain country.
usfs:ga-chattahoochee-oconee-national-fo.dontMiss: The forest connects to a vast web of neighboring public land: it borders the Cherokee, Nantahala, and Sumter National Forests, with the Pisgah just 13 miles off. On the water, Lake Sidney Lanier stretches across 155 km2, formed by Buford Dam on the Chattahoochee in 1958 and served by three campgrounds and facilities, while West Point Lake adds four more.
usfs:ga-chattahoochee-oconee-national-fo.whenToGo: Autumn is typically the driest stretch: October and November average just 6 wet days each, with highs around 72 and 63°F. Spring runs mild too, with April typically near 72°F. Midsummer is warmest and wettest—July averages 88°F highs and 19 wet days.
usfs:ga-chattahoochee-oconee-national-fo.whereToBase: Several towns sit inside the forest boundary. Morganton and Young Harris lie in the northern mountains, while Dillard, Sky Valley, and Tallulah Falls anchor the eastern reaches near Rabun County—all placing you directly within the forest for easy access to its ridges and lakes.

usfs:id-boise-national-forest.whyCome: Boise National Forest spreads across more than 2.5 million acres of central Idaho, managed by the U.S. Forest Service as five ranger districts: Cascade, Emmett, Idaho City, Lowman, and Mountain Home. Established in 1908 from part of Sawtooth National Forest, it offers a vast stretch of mountain country for those drawn to open, uncrowded terrain.
usfs:id-boise-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits amid a rare cluster of public land, sharing boundaries with Payette, Salmon-Challis, and Sawtooth National Forests — an unbroken landscape stretching for millions of acres. Its reservoirs reward exploring: Deadwood Reservoir, formed by the 1931 Deadwood Dam, has four campgrounds and a named recreation area, while Lake Cascade covers roughly 101 square kilometers behind the Bureau of Reclamation's 1948 Cascade Dam.
usfs:id-boise-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clearest window: July typically sees highs near 83°F and just 2 wet days, with August close behind at 80°F and 5 wet days. September stays pleasant, typically around 72°F with 5 wet days. Winter months like December bring highs in the mid-30s and as many as 17 wet days.
usfs:id-boise-national-forest.whereToBase: Idaho City sits just 0.2 miles from the boundary, with Cascade at 0.3 miles and Placerville at 0.4 miles — all close enough for easy access. Crouch and Donnelly, each about 2.8 miles out, round out the options for travelers wanting a slightly wider spread of small-town bases.

usfs:id-caribou-targhee-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than 2.63 million acres of Idaho, Wyoming and a sliver of Utah, Caribou-Targhee National Forest fans out in several separate sections. To the east it meets Yellowstone and Grand Teton national parks, and most of the forest belongs to the 20-million-acre Greater Yellowstone Ecosystem — a landscape of high country, water and wildlife habitat.
usfs:id-caribou-targhee-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders Yellowstone and Grand Teton national parks and connects to a web of neighboring forests, including Bridger-Teton and Beaverhead-Deerlodge. Among its waters, natural Bear Lake spans 283.3 sq km with its own named recreation area, while Jackson Lake offers five campgrounds and facilities. Palisades Reservoir, held back by the 1957 Palisades Dam on the South Fork Snake River, adds two more.
usfs:id-caribou-targhee-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clear window: July typically runs warm and dry, with highs near 88°F, lows around 62°F and just 2 wet days. June is nearly as dry at about 4 wet days and a 76°F high. September stays mild, typically near 74°F with roughly 4 wet days.
usfs:id-caribou-targhee-national-forest.whereToBase: Alpine and Island Park both sit inside the forest, right at the boundary. Soda Springs lies just outside at 0.1 miles from the edge, with Ashton and Dubois close behind at about 0.2 miles from the boundary — all handy for reaching different sections of the forest.

usfs:id-idaho-panhandle-national-forests.whyCome: Spanning more than 2.5 million acres of public land across the Idaho panhandle, these three combined forests — the former Kaniksu, Coeur d'Alene, and St. Joe — stretch north to the Canadian border, with small reaches into eastern Washington and western Montana. Administered from Coeur d'Alene, this is big, water-laced northern country made for unhurried exploration.
usfs:id-idaho-panhandle-national-forests.dontMiss: The forest shares open boundaries with four neighbors — Colville, Kootenai, Lolo, and Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forests — creating a vast contiguous expanse of wild land. Among its flagship waters, Lake Pend Oreille covers 375 square kilometers, backed by the 1955 Albeni Falls Dam. Priest Lake offers three campgrounds and facilities, while Lake Koocanusa has two.
usfs:id-idaho-panhandle-national-forests.whenToGo: Summer is the clearest, driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 79°F, lows around 56°F, and just 4 wet days, with August close behind at 76°F and 6 wet days. Winters run cold and damp — January highs hover near 30°F with 12 wet days.
usfs:id-idaho-panhandle-national-forests.whereToBase: Several small towns sit right against the boundary. Fernan Lake Village and Saint Maries are each about 0.1 miles out, East Hope roughly 0.3 miles, and Hope near 0.4 miles — all convenient jumping-off points into the forest's northern and southern reaches.

usfs:id-nez-perce-clearwater-national-fo.whyCome: Spread across more than 4 million acres of Idaho backcountry, the Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forests offer a vast expanse of mountains, rivers, and reservoirs. This is deep-woods territory, where forested ridgelines and river canyons stretch in nearly every direction, inviting those looking to lose themselves in one of the largest wild landscapes in the northern Rockies.
usfs:id-nez-perce-clearwater-national-fo.dontMiss: The forests sit within an enormous corridor of shared public land, bordering the Payette, Wallowa-Whitman, Idaho Panhandle, and Lolo National Forests, with the Salmon-Challis just nine miles away. At their heart lies Dworshak Reservoir, a 66.2-square-kilometer expanse held back by Dworshak Dam on the North Fork Clearwater River, completed in 1973 by the Army Corps of Engineers.
usfs:id-nez-perce-clearwater-national-fo.whenToGo: July is typically the driest and warmest window, with highs near 84°F and just 2 wet days, followed by August at 82°F and around 5 wet days. Winter runs cold and damp: January highs sit near 37°F with roughly 12 wet days, and December climbs to 19.
usfs:id-nez-perce-clearwater-national-fo.whereToBase: Kooskia and Kamiah both sit just 0.2 miles from the boundary, as does Elk River, making any of the three an easy jumping-off point. Pierce lies about 0.4 miles out, and Potlatch roughly 0.6 miles from the forest edge.

usfs:id-payette-national-forest.whyCome: Payette National Forest spreads across roughly 2.3 million acres of central western Idaho, in parts of Valley, Idaho, Adams, and Washington counties. Managed from McCall and divided into five ranger districts, this expanse of federally managed land sits amid some of the state's largest wild country, offering a broad landscape of forest, river, and reservoir to explore.
usfs:id-payette-national-forest.dontMiss: Payette borders extensive public land: the Hells Canyon National Recreation Area to the west, plus the Boise, Salmon-Challis, Wallowa-Whitman, and Nez Perce-Clearwater National Forests, each over two million acres. Deadwood Reservoir, formed by the 1931 Deadwood Dam on the Deadwood River, anchors a named recreation area with four campgrounds and facilities. Lake Cascade, the largest at 101.3 square kilometers, was created by 1948's Cascade Dam.
usfs:id-payette-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 74°F with lows around 47°F and just 6 wet days, and August stays warm at 70°F with 8 wet days. September remains pleasant, typically reaching 62°F. Winter months are cold and wet, with December averaging 19 wet days.
usfs:id-payette-national-forest.whereToBase: Council sits just outside the boundary, about 0.1 miles away, with McCall (0.2 miles) and New Meadows (0.3 miles) close behind. All three make convenient gateways. Riggins and Donnelly lie a little farther out, roughly 4.2 and 4.5 miles from the boundary.

usfs:id-salmon-challis-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning 4,235,940 acres in east-central Idaho, Salmon-Challis is one of the largest national forests in the lower 48 states. It holds most of the Frank Church–River of No Return Wilderness, the largest wilderness area south of Alaska. This is a landscape defined by sheer scale and remoteness, drawing travelers seeking rugged, roadless country.
usfs:id-salmon-challis-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares boundaries with a remarkable run of public land: Payette, Sawtooth, Caribou-Targhee, Beaverhead-Deerlodge and Boise National Forests all border it directly, with Nez Perce-Clearwater just nine miles off. For water, natural Redfish Lake anchors the recreation side with 17 campgrounds and facilities, while Deadwood Reservoir, formed by a 1931 Bureau of Reclamation dam, adds four more.
usfs:id-salmon-challis-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 79°F, lows around 52°F, and only 7 wet days, with August close behind at 74°F. September stays mild near 67°F with just 6 wet days. Winters are cold, with January highs around 23°F.
usfs:id-salmon-challis-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit right at the edge. Leadore and Salmon both lie about 0.1 miles from the boundary, while Challis and Mackay are roughly 0.3 miles out. Lost River rounds out the options at about a mile from the forest's edge.

usfs:id-sawtooth-national-forest.whyCome: Sprawling across roughly 2.1 million acres in Idaho and Utah, Sawtooth National Forest traces its origins to a 1905 proclamation by President Theodore Roosevelt. Its heart is the Sawtooth National Recreation Area, established in 1972, which shelters the Sawtooth, Cecil D. Andrus–White Clouds, and Hemingway–Boulders wilderness areas—a landscape of jagged peaks, wilderness, and alpine water.
usfs:id-sawtooth-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits amid a vast web of public land, sharing boundaries with Boise and Salmon-Challis National Forests and lying near the Caribou-Targhee, so exploration extends well past its edges. Natural Redfish Lake anchors recreation with 17 campgrounds and facilities. Nearby, the Bureau of Reclamation's American Falls and Anderson Ranch reservoirs on the Snake and South Fork Boise rivers add still more water.
usfs:id-sawtooth-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically runs a warm 93°F high with essentially no wet days, while August eases to 87°F with about 3 wet days. For milder shoulder-season conditions, September typically brings 79°F highs, 53°F lows, and only 2 wet days.
usfs:id-sawtooth-national-forest.whereToBase: Stanley sits inside the forest itself, an ideal jumping-off point. Ketchum and Fairfield both lie just 0.2 miles from the boundary, while Hailey is about 0.6 miles out and Sun Valley roughly 1.3 miles away—all within easy reach of the trailheads and lakes.

usfs:il-shawnee-national-forest.whyCome: Set in the Shawnee Hills of Southern Illinois, this national forest spans roughly 498,615 acres of federally managed land across nine counties. Administered by the U.S. Forest Service from headquarters in Harrisburg, it stands as the single largest publicly owned body of land in the state — a broad expanse of hill country that anchors the region.
usfs:il-shawnee-national-forest.dontMiss: Several man-made lakes shape the recreation here. Crab Orchard Lake, at 29.5 square kilometers behind a 1939 dam, offers a campground and a recreation area, while Little Grassy and Devils Kitchen Lakes each hold two camping facilities. To the northwest, the vast Mark Twain National Forest lies about 24 miles away, extending public land options for a longer regional trip.
usfs:il-shawnee-national-forest.whenToGo: Fall is typically the driest stretch: September and October average highs of 82°F and 71°F with just 7 wet days each. Spring warms comfortably, with May highs near 76°F, though April and May typically bring around 12 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs near 88°F.
usfs:il-shawnee-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit inside the forest itself, right at the boundary. Simpson, Golconda, and Goreville all lie within the forest with no distance to the edge, giving straightforward access to the hills, lakes, and trails around them.

usfs:in-hoosier-national-forest.whyCome: In the hills of southern Indiana, Hoosier National Forest spreads across four separate sections managed by the U.S. Forest Service. Among its notable sites are the Lick Creek Settlement Site, the Potts Creek Rockshelter Archeological Site, and the Jacob Rickenbaugh House. Headquarters sit in Bedford, with a regional office in Tell City.
usfs:in-hoosier-national-forest.dontMiss: Two man-made lakes anchor the forest's water recreation. Monroe Lake, the larger at 45 square kilometers, was formed by Monroe Dam on Salt Creek in 1965 and offers four campgrounds and facilities. Patoka Lake, at 36.9 square kilometers, was created by Patoka Lake Dam on the Patoka River in 1978. Both dams are operated by the USACE Louisville District.
usfs:in-hoosier-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is typically the driest stretch, with September and October averaging highs of 80 and 69 and just 7 wet days each. Spring warms through May, when highs typically reach 74, though April and May see around 12 wet days.
usfs:in-hoosier-national-forest.whereToBase: English sits inside the forest itself. Brownstown lies just 0.1 miles from the boundary, and Birdseye about 0.3 miles out. Shoals and Paoli are also within a mile, at 0.7 and 0.9 miles respectively.

usfs:ky-daniel-boone-national-forest.whyCome: Established in 1937 and renamed in 1966 for the explorer Daniel Boone, this Kentucky forest spans 708,000 acres of federally owned land within a 2,100,000-acre proclamation boundary. It gathers rugged terrain, several reservoirs, and a network of camping and recreation areas across the state's eastern uplands.
usfs:ky-daniel-boone-national-forest.dontMiss: Lake Cumberland, at roughly 205 square kilometers, is the forest's largest lake, held back by Wolf Creek Dam on the Cumberland River since 1951. Laurel River Lake counts five campgrounds and facilities, while Buckhorn Lake pairs a named recreation area with its dam on the Middle Fork Kentucky River. Cave Run Lake adds further reservoir shoreline.
usfs:ky-daniel-boone-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is typically the driest stretch, with September highs near 78°F and about 8 wet days, and October around 69°F with roughly 7 wet days. Spring warms from the 60s in April to mid-70s in May, though wetter, with 12 to 13 wet days.
usfs:ky-daniel-boone-national-forest.whereToBase: Several towns sit inside the forest boundary. Morehead lies near Cave Run Lake in the north, while Beattyville and Frenchburg anchor the central region. Buckhorn is set close to its namesake lake, making these convenient starting points for exploring the surrounding public land.

usfs:la-kisatchie-national-forest.whyCome: Louisiana's only national forest spreads across 1,063,029 acres of piney hills and hardwood bottoms in seven central and northern parishes. Part of the Cenozoic uplands, it holds vast stretches of longleaf pine and one of the largest natural landscapes in the state, with some 604,000 acres of public land supporting rare plants, animals, and habitats.
usfs:la-kisatchie-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest contains and buffers the Kisatchie Hills Wilderness, a nationally designated area protecting the coastal plain's biodiversity, alongside rare hillside seepage bogs and calcareous prairies. About 19 miles away lies the 454,542-acre Sabine National Forest, extending your options across two federal forests. Nearby waters include Catahoula Lake, a 66.9-square-kilometer man-made lake, and Lake Bistineau at 52.9 square kilometers.
usfs:la-kisatchie-national-forest.whenToGo: For the driest conditions, October typically sees highs near 81°F, lows around 60°F, and just 5 wet days. November follows with highs near 69°F and about 7 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July and August highs in the mid-90s°F.
usfs:la-kisatchie-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small communities sit inside the forest boundary, including Woodworth, McNary, and Hineston Ward Five, each within the forest itself. These serve as quiet jumping-off points for exploring the surrounding piney hills and hardwood bottoms.

usfs:mi-hiawatha-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 894,836 acres of Michigan's Upper Peninsula, Hiawatha National Forest is a working landscape where commercial logging shares ground with wild country. Administered by the U.S. Forest Service, the forest is physically split into two subunits, known as the Eastside and Westside, giving visitors two distinct corners of the peninsula to explore.
usfs:mi-hiawatha-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest's reach touches some of the largest freshwater lakes on the continent. Lake Superior spans roughly 82,002 square kilometers, with Lake Huron and Lake Michigan close behind at about 59,060 and 57,857 square kilometers. Inland, the natural Burt Lake and Mullett Lake add quieter water at roughly 70 and 68 square kilometers respectively.
usfs:mi-hiawatha-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the gentlest window: July typically sees highs near 76°F with lows around 59°F and about 12 wet days, while August runs slightly cooler at 74°F and 10 wet days. September stays pleasant, typically near 68°F with fewer wet days. Winters are cold, with January highs around 27°F.
usfs:mi-hiawatha-national-forest.whereToBase: Saint Ignace sits closest at 0.6 miles from the boundary, followed by Munising at 0.8 miles. Chatham lies about 1.1 miles out, with Manistique and Gladstone each roughly 1.3 miles from the forest edge. All make convenient jumping-off points for either subunit.

usfs:mi-huron-manistee-national-forests.whyCome: Two forests managed as one, the Huron–Manistee spans the northern Lower Peninsula of Michigan with 978,906 acres of public land, including 5,786 acres of wetlands. Organized into four districts and headquartered in Cadillac, it offers clean air and water, recreation, and habitat for fish and wildlife across a broad reach of woods, waterways, and shoreline country.
usfs:mi-huron-manistee-national-forests.dontMiss: The forests stretch between two of the Great Lakes, with Lake Huron and Lake Michigan among the largest waters near their edges, each covering roughly 57,000 to 59,000 square kilometers. Inland, natural lakes reward exploring: Torch Lake, Houghton Lake, and Higgins Lake add classic Michigan lake country to the mix of shoreline and interior scenery.
usfs:mi-huron-manistee-national-forests.whenToGo: Summer is the warm window, with July highs typically near 81°F and lows around 61°F, though rain falls on roughly 9 to 11 days a month. Early fall eases things: September typically brings highs near 74°F, lows around 54°F, and just about 7 wet days—the driest stretch of the year.
usfs:mi-huron-manistee-national-forests.whereToBase: Several small towns sit inside the forests, right at the action. Baldwin and White Cloud anchor the central Baldwin/White Cloud District, while Free Soil, Walkerville, and Harrietta offer additional footholds within the boundary for reaching lakes, rivers, and woodland trails.

usfs:mi-ottawa-national-forest.whyCome: Ottawa National Forest spreads across roughly 993,010 acres of Michigan's Upper Peninsula, reaching into Gogebic, Ontonagon, and slices of Iron, Houghton, Baraga, and Marquette counties. Under U.S. Forest Service jurisdiction, it's a vast expanse of northern woods, lakes, and rivers where the shoreline of Lake Superior meets one of the region's largest tracts of public land.
usfs:mi-ottawa-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken border with Chequamegon-Nicolet National Forest, adding more than two million adjacent acres of public land to roam. Water defines the landscape: Lac Vieux Desert, held by a 1907 dam on the Wisconsin River, offers a developed facility, while Lake Gogebic sits behind the 1906 Bergland Dam on the West Branch Ontonagon River.
usfs:mi-ottawa-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the gentlest window, with July typically reaching highs near 77°F and lows around 58°F, and roughly 12 wet days. September stays mild, with highs near 71°F and only about 9 wet days, historically the driest stretch. Winters run cold, with January highs around 23°F.
usfs:mi-ottawa-national-forest.whereToBase: Indian Village sits inside the forest itself. Wakefield lies just 0.3 miles from the boundary, with Ontonagon and Bessemer both about 0.5 miles out. Iron River is a short 2 miles from the edge, making any of these a workable jumping-off point.

usfs:mn-chippewa-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 1,598,139 acres of north central Minnesota, Chippewa National Forest covers parts of Itasca, Cass and Beltrami counties, with headquarters in Cass Lake. This is lake country at its most expansive, a landscape of forest and water where big natural lakes anchor the region and ranger stations in Blackduck, Deer River and Walker mark the way in.
usfs:mn-chippewa-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest holds some of Minnesota's largest lakes: Lower Red Lake at 666.5 square kilometers and Leech Lake at 418.2. Lake Winnibigoshish, 225.7 square kilometers, is shaped by the Winnibigoshish Dam on the Mississippi River, built in 1884. Cass Lake, though smaller at 83.8 square kilometers, carries seven campgrounds and facilities. Superior National Forest lies roughly 20 miles away.
usfs:mn-chippewa-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically brings highs near 77°F and lows around 63°F, with about 11 wet days. September stays mild, with highs near 70°F and lows around 57°F across roughly 9 wet days. Winters run cold, with January highs typically near 19°F.
usfs:mn-chippewa-national-forest.whereToBase: Several towns sit inside the forest boundary. Walker anchors the southwest near Leech Lake, while Longville and Remer lie to the east, and Zemple and Wahnena offer additional footholds within the forest itself.

usfs:mn-superior-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 3,886,761 acres in Minnesota's Arrowhead Region, Superior National Forest lies between the Canada–United States border and the north shore of Lake Superior. It forms part of the greater Boundary Waters region, a historic thoroughfare from the fur-trading days of New France and British North America.
usfs:mn-superior-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders Voyageurs National Park directly, putting 204,750 acres of park landscape within easy reach, with Isle Royale National Park roughly 21 miles off and Chippewa National Forest about 20 miles away. Lake Superior anchors the region at 82,002.3 square kilometers, while natural waters like Rainy Lake and Vermilion Lake add depth to the network.
usfs:mn-superior-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 76°F and lows around 58°F, with about 12 wet days. September stays comfortable, typically topping out near 69°F with roughly 9 wet days. Winters run cold, with January highs typically around 18°F.
usfs:mn-superior-national-forest.whereToBase: Every listed town sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Grand Marais anchors the Lake Superior shore, Ely serves the Boundary Waters, and Orr offers northern access. Hoyt Lakes and Goodwill round out the options, all within the forest's reach.

usfs:mo-mark-twain-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning roughly 3,071,296 acres across the southern half of Missouri, Mark Twain National Forest is a landscape of nine disconnected parcels named for the state's most famous author. Established on September 11, 1939, it covers 29 counties and represents 11% of all forested land in Missouri, with wilderness areas and National Scenic River corridors woven through six ranger districts.
usfs:mo-mark-twain-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits near Shawnee National Forest, about 24 miles away, expanding the public land within reach. Several large reservoirs anchor the region: Stockton Lake, a 101-square-kilometer impoundment behind Stockton Dam on the Sac River (1969), offers four campgrounds and facilities, while Table Rock Lake, formed by Table Rock Dam on the White River (1959), adds three more.
usfs:mo-mark-twain-national-forest.whenToGo: For mild, drier conditions, autumn is typically comfortable — October sees highs near 71°F, lows around 51°F, and just 7 wet days. Spring warms steadily, though May typically brings up to 16 wet days. Summer runs hot, with July highs near 88°F.
usfs:mo-mark-twain-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit inside the forest itself, including Centerville, Winona, and Newburg, each right on the boundary. All make practical starting points for exploring the surrounding ranger districts and reaching the forest's scattered parcels.

usfs:ms-de-soto-national-forest.whyCome: De Soto National Forest spreads across 518,587 acres of pine forest in southern Mississippi, named for 16th-century Spanish conquistador Hernando de Soto. It stands as one of the most important protected areas for the biological diversity of the Gulf Coast ecoregion of North America, a quiet expanse of longleaf and loblolly pine in the state's southern reaches.
