usfs:ms-de-soto-national-forest.whenToGo: October is typically the driest month, with highs near 81°F and only about 5 wet days. Late fall through early spring stays mild: January sees highs around 60°F. Summers run hot and wet, with July averaging highs of 91°F and roughly 22 wet days.

usfs:ms-homochitto-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across southwestern Mississippi, Homochitto National Forest covers 191,839 acres of woodland shaped by history. In the mid-1930s, the Civilian Conservation Corps began reforesting the area and building the roadways and recreation areas that still define it today, leaving a quietly worked landscape rather than a headline destination.
usfs:ms-homochitto-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest's centerpiece is Okhissa Lake, a 4.2-square-kilometer water body with a campground and facilities on its shore. It's the natural focus for a visit, offering a place to camp and settle in among the surrounding pine and hardwood country the CCC helped restore.
usfs:ms-homochitto-national-forest.whenToGo: Fall is the driest stretch: October typically sees highs near 80°F, lows around 59°F, and just 5 wet days. Spring offers milder heat, with April highs around 77°F and lows near 57°F, though roughly 11 wet days. Summer runs hot and humid, with July highs of 92°F.

usfs:mt-beaverhead-deerlodge-national-fo.whyCome: The largest of Montana's national forests, Beaverhead-Deerlodge spreads across 3.36 million acres in nine separate sections over eight counties in the state's southwestern corner. Named by Theodore Roosevelt in 1908 and merged in 1996, the forest has roots in early conservation of the Big Hole River watershed. Its headquarters sit in Dillon.
usfs:mt-beaverhead-deerlodge-national-fo.dontMiss: The forest shares unbroken boundaries with a remarkable ring of public land — the Salmon-Challis, Caribou-Targhee, Custer Gallatin, Helena-Lewis and Clark, and Lolo National Forests — with Yellowstone National Park just 12 miles off. Hebgen Lake, 50.2 square kilometers behind the 1915 Hebgen Dam on the Madison River, anchors six campgrounds and facilities, while Canyon Ferry Lake stretches to 132.8 square kilometers.
usfs:mt-beaverhead-deerlodge-national-fo.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically brings highs near 78°F with lows around 51°F and about 9 wet days, while August runs slightly cooler at 74°F. September stays mild in the upper 60s with only about 7 wet days. Late spring is wetter, with May averaging 16 wet days.
usfs:mt-beaverhead-deerlodge-national-fo.whereToBase: Sauls Claim sits inside the forest itself. Just outside, Lima lies about 0.1 miles from the boundary and Ennis roughly 0.2 miles out, both handy jumping-off points. Walkerville and Boulder each sit about half a mile from the forest edge.

usfs:mt-custer-gallatin-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than 3.4 million acres of southwest Montana, Custer Gallatin National Forest runs along the state's southern border and reaches into northwest Wyoming. Its terrain rises and falls across mountains, forest, and open water, making it one of the larger national forests in the region and a broad canvas for those drawn to high-country Montana.
usfs:mt-custer-gallatin-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken boundary with Yellowstone National Park's 2.2 million acres, along with the Beaverhead-Deerlodge, Caribou-Targhee, and Helena-Lewis and Clark national forests, and sits just a mile from Shoshone. Within reach is Hebgen Lake, a 50.2-square-kilometer reservoir on the Madison River behind Hebgen Dam (1915), backed by six campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:mt-custer-gallatin-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 88°F with just 3 wet days, and August around 85°F with 4. September stays mild, typically near 78°F with 4 wet days. Spring runs wetter, with May averaging around 12 wet days.
usfs:mt-custer-gallatin-national-forest.whereToBase: West Yellowstone sits just 0.2 miles from the boundary, making it the closest base. Red Lodge follows at 1.7 miles and Big Timber at 2.6 miles. Bozeman (3.9 miles) and Livingston (4.4 miles) offer larger-town options a short drive out.

usfs:mt-flathead-national-forest.whyCome: Flathead National Forest sprawls across 2,404,935 acres of western Montana, just south of Glacier National Park in Flathead County. Roughly 1 million of those acres are designated wilderness. Named for the Flathead Native Americans of the region, it offers vast, largely undeveloped country where forest gives way to lakes, rivers, and high protected backcountry.
usfs:mt-flathead-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares an unbroken border with Glacier National Park and three neighboring national forests — Kootenai, Helena-Lewis and Clark, and Lolo — stitching together millions of contiguous acres. On the water, Flathead Lake covers 494.7 square kilometers behind the 1938 Selis Ksanka Qlispe Dam, while Hungry Horse Reservoir spans 95.5 square kilometers behind its 1953 dam, with three campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:mt-flathead-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clear window: July typically sees highs near 73°F, lows around 47°F, and just 6 wet days — the driest month of the year. August and September stay mild, with highs of 69°F and 61°F and around 9 wet days each, offering a quieter shoulder to the season.
usfs:mt-flathead-national-forest.whereToBase: Columbia Falls and Whitefish both sit about 1.8 miles from the forest boundary, making them the closest bases. Kalispell lies roughly 2.7 miles out. All three fall just outside the forest, within easy reach of the northern lakes and Glacier country beyond.

usfs:mt-helena-lewis-and-clark-national-.whyCome: Spanning roughly 3.2 million acres across west-central Montana, Helena-Lewis and Clark National Forest is landscape on a grand scale. The land carries deep human history: for at least 8,000 to 10,000 years it was home to Native American peoples, and members of the Blackfeet, Sioux, Cheyenne, Flathead and Crow nations later used its territory for hunting and seasonal winter camps.
usfs:mt-helena-lewis-and-clark-national-.dontMiss: The forest shares its boundary with Glacier National Park and four national forests, including Beaverhead-Deerlodge and Custer Gallatin, placing it at the heart of a vast stretch of public land. Its waters are a highlight: Canyon Ferry Lake, the largest at 132.8 square kilometers, sits behind a 1954 Missouri River dam, while Hauser Lake offers a named recreation area with six campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:mt-helena-lewis-and-clark-national-.whenToGo: Summer is the warmest and driest window: July typically sees highs near 83°F with lows around 60°F and just 6 wet days, and September stays mild at roughly 73°F with 6 wet days. Spring runs cooler and wetter, with May averaging highs near 60°F and 13 wet days.
usfs:mt-helena-lewis-and-clark-national-.whereToBase: Neihart sits inside the forest itself. Just beyond the boundary, White Sulphur Springs lies about 0.1 mile out, while Stanford and Townsend each sit roughly 0.3 mile away. Harlowton, about 0.7 mile from the boundary, rounds out the nearby options.

usfs:mt-kootenai-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning some 2.2 million acres across far northwestern Montana and into the Idaho panhandle, Kootenai National Forest reaches all the way to the Canada–US border. Its centerpiece is the Cabinet Mountains Wilderness, about 53 percent of whose 94,272 acres lie within the forest. Headquartered in Libby, it's a vast, quiet expanse of mountains, rivers, and lakes.
usfs:mt-kootenai-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares unbroken boundaries with three neighbors — the Idaho Panhandle, Flathead, and Lolo National Forests — extending backcountry in every direction, and Glacier National Park sits just 10 miles away. Man-made Lake Koocanusa, held by Libby Dam on the Kootenai River (1973), covers roughly 117 square kilometers and offers two campgrounds and facilities. Noxon Reservoir, formed by Noxon Rapids Dam (1960), adds more shoreline.
usfs:mt-kootenai-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 81°F with just 2 wet days, and September stays mild around 69°F with about 3 wet days. Winters are cold and damp — December typically brings highs near 30°F and 16 wet days.
usfs:mt-kootenai-national-forest.whereToBase: Troy and Rexford both sit right at the boundary, about 0.1 miles out. Libby, the forest's headquarters town, is roughly 0.8 miles away, while Eureka lies about 1.1 miles from the edge — each an easy launch point into the surrounding country.

usfs:mt-lolo-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across roughly 2.6 million acres of western Montana, Lolo National Forest runs to the Idaho line west of the Continental Divide. It holds four wilderness areas—Scapegoat and Selway-Bitterroot partially within, Welcome Creek and Rattlesnake entirely so. Assembled in 1906 from four earlier forests, its mix of continental and maritime weather yields an unusually diverse transitional forest.
usfs:mt-lolo-national-forest.dontMiss: Lolo shares boundaries with six national forests, including Nez Perce-Clearwater, Beaverhead-Deerlodge and Flathead, opening a vast web of connected public land. Among nearby waters, Hungry Horse Reservoir behind its 1953 Bureau of Reclamation dam offers three campgrounds and facilities, and natural Swan Lake adds three more—both good bases for lakeside camping in the region.
usfs:mt-lolo-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliably dry stretch: July typically sees highs near 82°F, lows around 56°F, and just 5 wet days, with September easing to 71°F and about 7 wet days. Winter turns cold and damp—January highs near 32°F and roughly 10 wet days.
usfs:mt-lolo-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit right at the forest's edge: Superior (0.1 miles out), Thompson Falls (0.2), and Alberton (0.3). Plains lies 0.5 miles from the boundary, while Missoula, about 2.7 miles away, offers the largest hub for supplies and services before heading in.

usfs:nc-croatan-national-forest.whyCome: Set on the Atlantic coast of North Carolina, Croatan National Forest was established on July 29, 1936, and stretches across 307,501 acres administered by the U.S. Forest Service. Managed alongside North Carolina's other national forests from headquarters in Asheville, Croatan keeps a local ranger district office in New Bern, anchoring a distinctly coastal corner of the forest system.
usfs:nc-croatan-national-forest.dontMiss: Catfish Lake is the forest's flagship water, covering 3.8 square kilometers, with a campground and facilities on hand for anyone planning to linger by the shore.
usfs:nc-croatan-national-forest.whenToGo: Fall is typically the driest stretch: October averages highs near 73°F with lows around 57°F and just 6 wet days, and December sees similar dryness with highs of 59°F. April is another comfortable window, typically reaching 73°F with about 7 wet days.

usfs:nc-nantahala-national-forest.whyCome: The largest of North Carolina's four national forests, Nantahala spreads across 1,320,763 acres of western mountains and valleys. Its Cherokee name means "Land of the Noonday Sun," earned where sunlight reaches deep gorge floors only at midday. As the second wettest region in the continental US after the Pacific Northwest, it forms part of the Appalachian temperate rainforest, with the Nantahala River running through it.
usfs:nc-nantahala-national-forest.dontMiss: Few forests connect to so much protected land: Nantahala borders Great Smoky Mountains National Park and the Pisgah, Cherokee, Chattahoochee-Oconee, and Sumter national forests, all directly adjoining. Its flagship waters are all man-made reservoirs, including Lake Keowee at 68.3 square kilometers and Tellico Lake at 61.3. Chatuge Lake, held by TVA's Chatuge Dam since 1942, offers four campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:nc-nantahala-national-forest.whenToGo: For drier, mild conditions, October typically brings highs near 59°F, lows around 42°F, and only about 9 wet days, matched by November. Spring's April is comfortable, with highs near 58°F and roughly 10 wet days. Summer runs wettest, with July averaging 21 wet days.

usfs:nc-pisgah-national-forest.whyCome: Pisgah National Forest spreads across 1,160,338 acres of the Appalachian Mountains in western North Carolina. Administered by the U.S. Forest Service from common headquarters in Asheville, it is managed alongside North Carolina's three other national forests, with ranger district offices in Pisgah Forest, Mars Hill, and Nebo. It rewards travelers who come for high-country mountains and quiet forest.
usfs:nc-pisgah-national-forest.dontMiss: Pisgah sits within a rare web of connected public land: it directly borders Great Smoky Mountains National Park (519,621 acres), Cherokee National Forest, and Nantahala National Forest, with Sumter National Forest just 3 miles away. Watauga Lake, a 25.7-square-kilometer reservoir formed by TVA's 1948 Watauga Dam on the Watauga River, offers six campgrounds and facilities lakeside.
usfs:nc-pisgah-national-forest.whenToGo: October is typically the driest month, with highs near 60°F, lows around 44°F, and about 10 wet days. November stays similarly dry, roughly 9 wet days with highs near 51°F. Midsummer runs wetter, with July averaging 22 wet days despite highs around 76°F.

usfs:nc-uwharrie-national-forest.whyCome: Set across the rolling hills of south central North Carolina, Uwharrie is the smallest of the state's four national forests, spanning about 50,645 acres primarily in Montgomery County. Its centerpiece is the Birkhead Mountains Wilderness, a 5,025-acre officially designated wilderness that offers quiet backcountry within an easy reach of the Piedmont.
usfs:nc-uwharrie-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest holds two sizable man-made lakes. Badin Lake, covering 22.5 square kilometers behind the Yadkin Narrows dam on the Yadkin River (built 1917), anchors the recreation here with seven campgrounds and facilities. Nearby Lake Tillery, at 19.6 square kilometers, sits behind the 1928 Tillery dam on the Pee Dee River.
usfs:nc-uwharrie-national-forest.whenToGo: Autumn is typically the driest stretch, with October averaging highs near 72°F, lows around 53°F, and only about 5 wet days. For milder green-season conditions, April typically brings highs of 73°F and roughly 7 wet days, before summer heat and rain build.

usfs:nh-white-mountain-national-forest.whyCome: Set within the White Mountains of New Hampshire, this forest was established in 1918 following the Weeks Act of 1911, with land acquisition beginning in 1914. Conservationist Katherine Sleeper Walden helped secure at-risk land, including thousands of acres around the locally famous Bowl near Wonalancet. Most of its terrain lies in New Hampshire, with a small portion extending into neighboring Maine.
usfs:nh-white-mountain-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest spans hundreds of thousands of acres, and just two miles away sits the Green Mountain and Finger Lakes National Forests, another 854,081 acres of public land within easy reach. Nearby waters range from the vast Lake Winnipesaukee at 184.8 square kilometers to natural gems like Umbagog Lake and Squam Lake, offering broad scope for exploring open water.
usfs:nh-white-mountain-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is typically pleasant, with September highs near 65°F, lows around 49°F, and about 10 wet days. For crisp winter conditions, January typically brings highs of 24°F and lows near 9°F with roughly 12 wet days. Summer runs warmer but wetter, with July highs of 73°F.
usfs:nh-white-mountain-national-forest.whereToBase: Berlin sits inside the forest itself, right at the boundary. For nearby options, Riley lies about 1.6 miles from the boundary, while Sanford is around 3 miles out. Each puts you within short reach of the forest's trails and waters.

usfs:nm-carson-national-forest.whyCome: Carson National Forest spreads across roughly 1.6 million acres of northern New Mexico, encompassing 6,070 square kilometers of high country administered under a mixed-use policy that balances recreation, grazing, and resource extraction. It's a vast landscape where mountains, reservoirs, and forest meet, offering room to roam far from the crowds.
usfs:nm-carson-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares boundaries with Santa Fe and Rio Grande National Forests, with San Juan National Forest just 10 miles off, stitching Carson into an enormous corridor of public land. It also holds five reservoirs: Navajo (62.2 sq km) behind the 1963 Navajo Dam, plus Heron, Abiquiu, El Vado, and Eagle Nest, El Vado's dam dating to 1935.
usfs:nm-carson-national-forest.whenToGo: Late spring into early summer is pleasant, with April highs near 52°F and only about 5 wet days, warming to June's 73°F. Summer brings the wettest stretch, typically 16 wet days in July. Autumn is mild and drier, with October highs around 57°F and roughly 7 wet days.
usfs:nm-carson-national-forest.whereToBase: Red River sits inside the forest, right at the boundary, making it a natural launch point for exploring the high country and nearby reservoirs.

usfs:nm-cibola-national-forest.whyCome: Cibola National Forest scatters across central and northern New Mexico, reaching into west Texas and Oklahoma. Its name traces to a Zuni word for their pueblos and tribal lands, later read by the Spanish as "buffalo." Spanning the Sandia, Manzano, Mt. Taylor, Magdalena and other ranges, the forest climbs from 5,000 to 11,301 feet across four ranger districts.
usfs:nm-cibola-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest holds 137,701 acres of designated Wilderness plus 246,000 acres of Inventoried Roadless Areas. Nearby public lands widen the horizon: Gila National Forest lies about 4 miles off, Lincoln 11 miles, and Santa Fe 19 miles. Lake Meredith, a 66-square-kilometer reservoir behind Sanford Dam on the Canadian River, anchors a named recreation area with two campgrounds and two rec areas.
usfs:nm-cibola-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring and fall offer the mildest going. April typically brings highs near 72°F with lows around 46°F and just two wet days; October runs about 73°F by day and 50°F at night with four wet days. Summer heats up, with July highs near 92°F.
usfs:nm-cibola-national-forest.whereToBase: Tijeras sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Grants and Magdalena lie just 0.6 miles out, while Mountainair and Corona are about 1.1 miles from the edge — each a practical starting point for reaching the forest's far-flung districts.

usfs:nm-gila-national-forest.whyCome: Established in 1905, Gila National Forest spreads across roughly 2,710,659 acres, making it the sixth largest national forest in the continental United States. Its enduring draw is history as much as terrain: the Gila Wilderness, set aside in 1924, was the country's first designated wilderness. The Aldo Leopold and Blue Range Wildernesses lie within its borders, adding to a vast, layered landscape.
usfs:nm-gila-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest also administers 614,202 acres of the Apache National Forest in New Mexico, bringing the total under Gila management to about 3.3 million acres. It shares a boundary with the Apache-Sitgreaves National Forests and sits near the Cibola National Forest, about four miles off, so a single trip can string together enormous stretches of contiguous and neighboring public land.
usfs:nm-gila-national-forest.whenToGo: Spring is typically dry and mild: April sees highs near 63°F with just 2 wet days, May around 72°F with 3. Autumn returns the calm, with October highs near 66°F and 5 wet days. Midsummer brings monsoon moisture, with July and August averaging 14 to 15 wet days.
usfs:nm-gila-national-forest.whereToBase: Reserve sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Santa Clara also lies right at the edge, while Silver City is about 0.6 miles out and Bayard roughly 1.5 miles from the boundary, giving several nearby options for setting out into the forest.

usfs:nm-lincoln-national-forest.whyCome: Lincoln National Forest spreads across roughly 1.1 million acres of southern New Mexico, reaching from near the Texas border across four counties. Established by Presidential Proclamation in 1902, it gathers three major mountain ranges — the Sacramento, Guadalupe, and Capitan — along with terrain shifting from desert to forested peaks and sub-alpine grasslands, plus protected wilderness and winter sports areas.
usfs:nm-lincoln-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest's position among public lands is a real draw: it directly borders Guadalupe Mountains National Park and Carlsbad Caverns National Park, with White Sands National Park about 12 miles away and the vast Cibola National Forest roughly 11 miles off. Together these neighbors let you pair high forested ranges with caverns, dunes, and desert within one trip.
usfs:nm-lincoln-national-forest.whenToGo: For mild, drier conditions, October is typically pleasant with highs around 70°F, lows near 46°F, and about 5 wet days. Late spring—April—runs highs near 69°F and just 3 wet days. Midsummer is warmest and wettest, with August highs around 81°F and roughly 10 wet days.
usfs:nm-lincoln-national-forest.whereToBase: Ruidoso and neighboring Ruidoso Downs both sit inside the forest, as does Cloudcroft, making all three convenient mountain bases. If you prefer a town just outside the boundary, Capitan lies about 0.9 miles out, while Alamogordo — home to the forest headquarters — is roughly 2.5 miles from the edge.

usfs:nm-santa-fe-national-forest.whyCome: Santa Fe National Forest spreads across more than 1.5 million acres of northern New Mexico, established in 1915. Elevations climb from 5,300 feet to the 13,103-foot summit of Truchas Peak in the Pecos Wilderness. Spanning the Jemez Mountains and the Sangre de Cristo range, it offers high desert, forested canyons, and alpine country within reach of Santa Fe.
usfs:nm-santa-fe-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a boundary with the 1.5-million-acre Carson National Forest, with Cibola National Forest roughly 19 miles away, extending the wild country in every direction. On the water, Abiquiu Reservoir covers 13.2 square kilometers behind Abiquiu Dam (1963) on the Rio Chama, while El Vado Reservoir spreads 12.6 square kilometers behind a 1935 dam and offers three recreation areas.
usfs:nm-santa-fe-national-forest.whenToGo: Late spring is typically comfortable, with May highs near 76°F, lows around 50°F, and about five wet days. Autumn is another steady window: October typically sees highs near 69°F, lows around 45°F, and just three wet days. Summer runs warm, with July highs near 89°F.
usfs:nm-santa-fe-national-forest.whereToBase: Jemez Springs sits inside the forest at the boundary itself, a fitting hub for the Jemez Mountains. Just outside, Pecos lies 0.2 miles from the edge near the Sangre de Cristo country, while Santa Fe—home to the forest headquarters—is only 0.4 miles out, with Las Vegas 0.6 miles away.

usfs:nv-humboldt-toiyabe-national-forest.whyCome: Humboldt–Toiyabe is the principal national forest of Nevada, spilling west into eastern California, and at 6,289,821 acres it stands as the largest national forest reservation outside Alaska. Its scale spans desert basins, mountain ranges, and high-country lakes, giving travelers an enormous canvas of public land to explore across two states.
usfs:nv-humboldt-toiyabe-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares direct boundaries with Yosemite, Stanislaus, Tahoe, Eldorado, and Inyo National Forests, and sits near Great Basin (6 miles) and Death Valley (15 miles) — a rare corridor of connected public land. Within reach lie major waters: Lake Mead (541.3 km²), held by Hoover Dam on the Colorado River since 1935, and Lake Tahoe (497.7 km²).
usfs:nv-humboldt-toiyabe-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer typically brings the driest, warmest stretch: June highs near 83°F with 0 wet days, and July around 91°F with just 1. For milder conditions, May typically averages highs of 70°F with about 3 wet days, and September near 78°F with 2.
usfs:nv-humboldt-toiyabe-national-forest.whereToBase: Reno sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Ely lies just outside, 0.7 miles from the edge, and Sparks is close by at 3.4 miles. Farther out, Yerington (8.3 miles) and Elko (16.7 miles) offer additional starting points.

usfs:oh-wayne-national-forest.whyCome: Wayne National Forest is the first and only national forest in Ohio, spread across 856,185 acres of the Appalachian Unglaciated Allegheny Plateau. Its headquarters sit between The Plains and Nelsonville on US Route 33, overlooking the Hocking River. This is Ohio's corner of the Appalachians, a landscape of plateau country and quiet water.
usfs:oh-wayne-national-forest.dontMiss: Two man-made lakes anchor the forest. Senecaville Lake covers 14.1 square kilometers, held back by Senecaville Dam on the Seneca Fork of Wills Creek, completed in 1937 and run by the USACE Huntington District; it has a named recreation area. Burr Oak Reservoir, 2.6 square kilometers behind the 1950 Tom Jenkins Dam, adds a campground and recreation area.
usfs:oh-wayne-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is comfortable and drier: September typically sees highs near 79°F, lows around 59°F, and only 8 wet days, with October cooler at 68°F and 7 wet days. Summer runs warm, with July highs around 87°F and roughly 14 wet days.
usfs:oh-wayne-national-forest.whereToBase: All the nearest towns sit inside the forest itself. Nelsonville lies near the headquarters on US Route 33, while Murray City, Buchtel, Corning, and Rendville also fall within the boundary, putting you right among the plateau's wooded ridges and waterways.

usfs:or-deschutes-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning roughly 1.6 million acres along the east side of the Cascade Range in Central Oregon, Deschutes National Forest was established in 1908. It stretches across four counties, threaded by the Deschutes River and dotted with reservoirs and natural lakes. This is high-country forest terrain where the mountains meet the drier eastern slopes.
usfs:or-deschutes-national-forest.dontMiss: Deschutes shares borders with several forests, including the Fremont-Winema, Umpqua, Willamette, and Ochoco National Forests, and sits within 12 miles of Crater Lake National Park. For water, Wickiup Reservoir is the largest here at 41.2 square kilometers, fed by the Deschutes River behind the 1949 Bureau of Reclamation dam; Crane Prairie and Crescent Lake add named recreation areas with numerous campgrounds.
usfs:or-deschutes-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs of 85°F, lows of 54°F, and just 2 wet days, with August close behind at 80°F. For milder conditions, June brings highs near 72°F and 6 wet days. Winter runs cold and damp, with December averaging 17 wet days.
usfs:or-deschutes-national-forest.whereToBase: Sisters sits inside the forest itself. Bend lies just half a mile from the boundary, and La Pine is about 1.2 miles out. Farther afield, Culver and Metolius sit roughly 10 to 12 miles from the forest edge.

usfs:or-fremont-winema-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning more than 2.8 million acres of southern Oregon, the Fremont–Winema National Forest reaches from the crest of the Cascade Range past Lakeview to the California border. Formed in the 2002 merger of the Fremont and Winema forests, it wraps around lakes, high desert, and mountain country, with Klamath Falls the only sizable city nearby and headquarters in Lakeview.
usfs:or-fremont-winema-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a boundary with Crater Lake National Park and adjoins several other national forests, including Deschutes, Modoc, Umpqua, and Rogue River–Siskiyou, opening a vast stretch of connected public land. Within its reach lie striking waters: natural Goose Lake at 376.6 square kilometers, and Upper Klamath Lake, held by the 1921 Link River Diversion Dam.
usfs:or-fremont-winema-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest window: July typically sees highs near 86°F with just 2 wet days, and August around 81°F. Late spring warms up too, with June highs near 73°F and 7 wet days. Winter runs cold and damp, with December and January highs in the high 30s and 12 to 13 wet days.
usfs:or-fremont-winema-national-forest.whereToBase: Paisley sits just 0.2 miles from the boundary and Chiloquin about 0.7 miles out, both close-in options. Lakeview, roughly 1.8 miles away, doubles as the forest headquarters town, while Klamath Falls—the largest nearby city—lies about 11.4 miles from the boundary.

usfs:or-malheur-national-forest.whyCome: Malheur National Forest spreads across more than 1.4 million acres of the Blue Mountains in eastern Oregon, a landscape of high desert grasslands, sage, juniper, pine, and fir. Elevations climb from about 4,000 feet to the 9,038-foot summit of Strawberry Mountain, whose namesake range runs east to west through the forest's center.
usfs:or-malheur-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits shoulder to shoulder with two vast neighbors, the Umatilla and Wallowa-Whitman National Forests, with the Ochoco National Forest just three miles off, extending the wild country well beyond Malheur's own bounds. Phillips Lake, a natural lake, anchors a named recreation area with a campground, while reservoirs like Warm Springs and Beulah trace their dams back to the early 1900s.
usfs:or-malheur-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest, warmest window: July typically sees highs near 84°F with just one wet day, and August runs about 80°F with four. June and September stay mild in the 70s with around six wet days. Winter months are cold and damp, with December highs near 36°F.
usfs:or-malheur-national-forest.whereToBase: John Day and Hines both sit just 0.1 mile from the boundary, making either an easy jumping-off point. Prairie City lies about 0.2 mile out, and smaller Seneca sits 0.3 mile from the forest edge — all convenient bases for exploring the surrounding country.

usfs:or-mt-hood-national-forest.whyCome: Located 62 miles east of Portland, Mt. Hood National Forest extends south from the Columbia River Gorge across more than 60 miles of forested mountains, lakes and streams to the Olallie Scenic Area, beneath the slopes of Mount Jefferson. At its heart stands Mount Hood, a stratovolcano and the highest mountain in Oregon.
usfs:or-mt-hood-national-forest.dontMiss: This 1,060,160-acre forest shares an unbroken boundary with the Willamette National Forest, with Gifford Pinchot and Deschutes national forests within a short drive, opening a vast interconnected wilderness. Detroit Lake, formed by the 1953 Detroit Dam on the North Santiam River, anchors a named recreation area with nine campgrounds, while Timothy Lake adds eleven camping facilities of its own.
usfs:or-mt-hood-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 76°F with just 2 wet days, and August around 75°F with 5. September stays mild, typically near 68°F with 8 wet days. Winter months run wet, with December averaging 22 wet days and highs around 39°F.
usfs:or-mt-hood-national-forest.whereToBase: Dufur sits just outside the boundary, about 0.3 miles away, making it the closest base. Troutdale lies roughly 1 mile out, while Cascade Locks is about 1.8 miles from the forest edge and offers gateway access to the Columbia River Gorge.

usfs:or-ochoco-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across roughly 850,000 acres in the Ochoco Mountains of Central Oregon, north and east of Prineville, this national forest gathers rimrock, canyons, geologic oddities, dense pine forests, and high desert terrain. It also holds the headwaters of the North Fork Crooked River, and a 1993 study estimated some 95,000 acres of old growth within its bounds.
usfs:or-ochoco-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares its edge with the vast Deschutes National Forest and sits just three miles from Malheur, placing it within a broad web of Central Oregon public land. Two man-made reservoirs anchor the water recreation: Prineville Reservoir, at 11.6 square kilometers behind the 1961 Arthur R. Bowman Dam on the Crooked River, and Ochoco Reservoir, fed by Ochoco Creek behind a 1920 dam.
usfs:or-ochoco-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July and August typically see highs of 85 and 82, lows in the low 50s, and just 2 wet days per month. Late spring, around May, is cooler, with highs near 62 and about 10 wet days. Winters run cold and damp.
usfs:or-ochoco-national-forest.whereToBase: Prineville sits just 0.8 miles from the boundary and is home to the forest headquarters. Metolius lies 0.9 miles out and Culver about 1.5 miles, with Madras a bit farther at 2.9 miles, giving several nearby options along the forest's western approaches.

usfs:or-rogue-river-siskiyou-national-fo.whyCome: Spanning nearly 1.85 million acres across Oregon and California, the Rogue River–Siskiyou National Forest reaches from the crest of the Cascade Range west into the Siskiyou Mountains. Formed in 2004 by combining two formerly separate forests, it covers almost 2,800 square miles of varied terrain, with headquarters in Medford, Oregon.
usfs:or-rogue-river-siskiyou-national-fo.dontMiss: The forest borders Crater Lake National Park and connects to a broad web of public land, including the Klamath, Fremont-Winema, Six Rivers, and Umpqua National Forests. Within reach are natural Crater Lake and Diamond Lake, plus Lost Creek Lake, a man-made reservoir formed by the 1976 William L. Jess Dam on the Rogue River, with two recreation areas.
usfs:or-rogue-river-siskiyou-national-fo.whenToGo: July and August are typically the driest months, with highs near 92°F, mild lows in the upper 50s, and only about 1 wet day per month. Late spring, especially May and June, is typically warm and dry too, with highs from 71°F to 81°F and just 4 to 5 wet days.
usfs:or-rogue-river-siskiyou-national-fo.whereToBase: Butte Falls and Powers sit essentially on the boundary (0 and 0.6 miles). Cave Junction lies just 0.4 miles out, Ashland about 1.3 miles, and Gold Beach roughly 1.7 miles from the forest edge, giving a spread of options from inland valleys to the coast.

usfs:or-siuslaw-national-forest.whyCome: Established in 1908, Siuslaw National Forest spreads across 835,600 acres of western Oregon, spanning a striking range of ecosystems from coastal forests to sand dunes. It's a place where the Pacific edge meets deep timber, offering variety within a single forest that few other landscapes match.
usfs:or-siuslaw-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest holds several man-made lakes worth seeking out. Fern Ridge Lake, at 26.6 square kilometers, was formed by the 1941 Fern Ridge Dam on the Long Tom River and anchors a named recreation area with two campgrounds. Siltcoos Lake (13.3 sq km) counts six campgrounds and facilities, while Tahkenitch Lake adds three more. Umpqua National Forest lies roughly 13 miles away.
usfs:or-siuslaw-national-forest.whenToGo: July and August are typically the driest and warmest stretch, with highs of 77°F and just 1 to 3 wet days per month. June also tends to stay mild, with highs near 69°F and about 8 wet days. Expect frequent rain from November through March.
usfs:or-siuslaw-national-forest.whereToBase: Several coastal towns sit just outside the boundary. Dunes City and Reedsport are both about 0.3 miles out, while Waldport lies roughly 0.4 miles away. Yachats and Lakeside each sit about 0.6 miles from the forest edge.

usfs:or-umatilla-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across 1.4 million acres of the Blue Mountains in northeast Oregon and southeast Washington, Umatilla National Forest reaches into ten counties, from Umatilla and Grant to Walla Walla, with more than three-quarters of its terrain lying in Oregon. It's a broad, high-country expanse of mountain forest straddling two states.
usfs:or-umatilla-national-forest.dontMiss: Umatilla sits directly against two large neighbors, the 2,525,293-acre Wallowa-Whitman National Forest and the 1,786,541-acre Malheur National Forest, forming a vast stretch of connected public land. Along the Columbia, the man-made Lake Umatilla spans 202.5 square kilometers behind the John Day Lock and Dam. Natural Phillips Lake anchors a named recreation area with campgrounds.
usfs:or-umatilla-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest and warmest window: July typically sees highs near 85°F and just one wet day, with August close behind at 82°F and two wet days. Early fall stays pleasant, with September highs around 73°F and only four wet days.
usfs:or-umatilla-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit near the boundary. Granite, Heppner, and Ukiah each lie roughly 0.2 miles from the forest edge, while nearby Greenhorn is about 0.3 miles out. Larger Pendleton stands about 2.3 miles from the boundary.

usfs:or-umpqua-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than a million acres of southern Oregon's Cascade Range, Umpqua National Forest reaches through Douglas, Lane, and Jackson counties. Organized into four ranger districts—Cottage Grove, Diamond Lake, North Umpqua, and Tiller—the forest borders Crater Lake National Park and offers a broad, forested corner of the Pacific Northwest to explore.
usfs:or-umpqua-national-forest.dontMiss: Umpqua sits at the meeting point of a remarkable network of public land: it borders Crater Lake National Park and the Deschutes, Fremont-Winema, Willamette, and Rogue River-Siskiyou national forests. Crescent Lake, at 15.5 km², anchors a named recreation area with 12 campgrounds and facilities, while natural Waldo Lake (24.5 km²) and Odell Lake add further shorelines to explore.
usfs:or-umpqua-national-forest.whenToGo: July and August are typically the driest and warmest stretch, with highs around 82°F and 80°F and just 1 to 2 wet days per month. June, typically reaching 70°F with about 6 wet days, makes a milder shoulder option before summer fully arrives.
usfs:or-umpqua-national-forest.whereToBase: Roseburg sits closest, about 1.8 miles from the boundary and home to the forest's headquarters. Westfir lies roughly 4.4 miles out, and Oakridge about 5.8 miles from the boundary, giving several nearby options for arranging your visit.

usfs:or-wallowa-whitman-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across more than 2.5 million acres in northeastern Oregon and edging into Idaho, Wallowa-Whitman was formed in 1954 from the merger of the Wallowa and Whitman national forests. It takes its name from the Wallowa band of the Nez Perce people and missionaries Marcus and Narcissa Whitman. Forest headquarters sit in Baker City.
usfs:or-wallowa-whitman-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares unbroken boundaries with four other national forests — Payette, Umatilla, Malheur, and the vast Nez Perce-Clearwater — opening onto millions of adjoining public acres. Along the Snake River, Brownlee Reservoir stretches across 50.7 square kilometers behind its 1958 dam, while Phillips Lake, a natural 9.1-square-kilometer lake, anchors a named recreation area with a campground.
usfs:or-wallowa-whitman-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clear window: July typically brings highs near 76°F with just 3 wet days, and August warms to about 73°F with 6 wet days. September stays pleasant around 65°F. Winter runs cold and damp, with December highs near 31°F and 19 wet days.
usfs:or-wallowa-whitman-national-forest.whereToBase: Granite sits inside the forest at the boundary itself. Just outside, Greenhorn lies 0.1 miles out and Enterprise and Sumpter each about 0.4 miles from the boundary, with Unity roughly 0.8 miles away — a handful of small gateway communities on the forest's edges.

usfs:or-willamette-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning the central Cascade Range, Willamette National Forest covers more than 1,678,000 acres of Oregon high country. Over 380,000 acres are designated wilderness, taking in seven major mountain peaks and several National Wild and Scenic Rivers. The forest is named for the Willamette River, which rises within its bounds — headwaters to headwater country, all in one sweep.
usfs:or-willamette-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares direct boundaries with Deschutes, Mt. Hood, and Umpqua National Forests, and lies within 20 miles of Crater Lake National Park — a rare corridor of continuous public land. Anglers and paddlers gravitate to its reservoirs: Wickiup Reservoir (41.2 km²) and Crane Prairie Reservoir, both fed by the Deschutes River, plus natural Waldo Lake and Crescent Lake, each with named recreation areas.
usfs:or-willamette-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clear window: July typically sees highs near 83°F with just one wet day, and August around 81°F with three. Late spring into June brings milder highs in the 60s and 70s and fewer showers, while winter months are cool and wet, with 16 to 20 wet days.
usfs:or-willamette-national-forest.whereToBase: Several small towns sit inside the forest, including Westfir, Detroit, Oakridge, and Idanha, all right at the boundary. Sweet Home lies just outside, about 0.7 miles from the forest edge, offering another approach from the west.

usfs:pa-allegheny-national-forest.whyCome: In northwestern Pennsylvania, about 100 miles northeast of Pittsburgh, the Allegheny National Forest spreads across more than 513,000 acres of woodland. At its heart lies Kinzua Dam, which impounds the Allegheny River to form the Allegheny Reservoir. The forest is served from its headquarters in Warren, with ranger stations in Marienville and Bradford anchoring its two corners.
usfs:pa-allegheny-national-forest.dontMiss: Water defines this forest. The Allegheny Reservoir, its largest at 49.5 square kilometers, was created by Kinzua Dam in 1965 and offers two campgrounds and facilities. Smaller but well-equipped, East Branch Clarion River Lake (behind a 1952 dam) and Tionesta Lake (behind a 1940 dam) each hold three campgrounds and a named recreation area, all managed by the USACE Pittsburgh District.
usfs:pa-allegheny-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is the sweet spot: September typically brings highs near 70°F with the driest stretch of the year at about 10 wet days, and October cools to the low 60s with roughly 11 wet days. Summer runs warm, with July highs around 78°F but wetter conditions.
usfs:pa-allegheny-national-forest.whereToBase: Clarendon sits right inside the forest at the boundary itself. Just outside, Tidioute and Tionesta (about half a mile out) put you close to the Allegheny River corridor, while Lewis Run (0.1 mile) and Kane (1 mile) round out the options along the forest's edge.

usfs:sc-francis-marion-national-forest.whyCome: Just north of Charleston, South Carolina, Francis Marion National Forest carries the name of the revolutionary war hero known to the British as the Swamp Fox. Its more than 416,000 acres lie entirely within the Middle Atlantic coastal forests ecoregion and the subtropical coniferous forest, a low-country landscape of pine and swamp shaped by the Carolina coastal plain.
usfs:sc-francis-marion-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest's flagship water is Lake Moultrie, a natural body covering roughly 230 square kilometers. Its broad expanse anchors the region's coastal-plain character and adds a sizable open-water dimension to a forest otherwise defined by pine woodlands and subtropical swamp.
usfs:sc-francis-marion-national-forest.whenToGo: Autumn is typically the driest stretch, with October and November averaging highs near 76 and 68 and only about 6 wet days each month. Early spring is another comfortable window: March and April typically bring highs of 71 to 77 with 8 to 9 wet days. Midsummer turns hot and wet.

usfs:sc-sumter-national-forest.whyCome: Sumter National Forest spreads across three distinct sections of western and central South Carolina, from the Piedmont to the Appalachian foothills of the Andrew Pickens district in Oconee County. Managed alongside the Francis Marion National Forest from headquarters in Columbia, its Enoree, Long Cane, and Andrew Pickens ranger districts together offer a quiet, layered introduction to the state's woodlands.
usfs:sc-sumter-national-forest.dontMiss: Sumter shares its boundary with the vast Chattahoochee-Oconee National Forests and the Nantahala National Forest, opening onto a broad stretch of connected public land, with Pisgah National Forest just three miles beyond. Nearby waters run deep: J. Strom Thurmond Reservoir covers 165.2 square kilometers with four campgrounds and facilities, while Hartwell Lake and Richard B. Russell Lake add further shoreline recreation.
usfs:sc-sumter-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is often the most settled window: October typically sees highs near 73°F, lows around 54°F, and just 5 wet days. Spring brings mild April days, typically reaching 74°F with lows near 52°F. Summers run humid and wetter, with July highs around 91°F.

usfs:sd-black-hills-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning more than 1.25 million acres across southwestern South Dakota and northeastern Wyoming, Black Hills National Forest is a sprawling expanse of forested uplands managed by the Forest Service, with headquarters in Custer. Ranger districts reach from Rapid City and Spearfish to Sundance, Wyoming, opening a landscape that rewards travelers willing to roam widely.
usfs:sd-black-hills-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares a border with Wind Cave National Park, letting you pair woodland driving with an underground destination. Its reservoirs are worth building a trip around: Pactola, on Rapid Creek behind the 1956 Pactola Dam, anchors a named recreation area with around ten campgrounds and facilities. Belle Fourche and Angostura reservoirs each hold a named recreation area as well.
usfs:sd-black-hills-national-forest.whenToGo: Late summer into early fall is the driest, most settled stretch: August typically sees highs near 73°F with about 9 wet days, and September eases to highs around 67°F with roughly 6 wet days. May is the wettest month, typically bringing about 19 wet days.
usfs:sd-black-hills-national-forest.whereToBase: Several towns sit inside the forest boundary. Custer, home to the forest headquarters, and Hot Springs both make practical anchors, while Keystone offers another option within the forest. Pactola and Pringle round out the interior choices for travelers wanting to stay close to the reservoirs and back roads.

usfs:tn-cherokee-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning parts of Tennessee and North Carolina, Cherokee National Forest was established on June 14, 1920, and covers well over a million acres of Appalachian ridgelines and river valleys. Managed by the U.S. Forest Service, it forms a vast, forested backdrop for anyone drawn to the southern mountains and the quiet, layered wildness they hold.
usfs:tn-cherokee-national-forest.dontMiss: Cherokee sits at the heart of a remarkable web of public land, sharing a boundary with Great Smoky Mountains National Park and four neighboring national forests, including the sprawling George Washington and Jefferson National Forests. Together they create one of the largest contiguous stretches of protected mountains in the East, letting you cross from forest to national park without ever leaving wild country.
usfs:tn-cherokee-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is comfortable and among the driest stretches: October typically sees highs near 70°F, lows around 49°F, and about 9 wet days. Spring offers milder warmth, with April highs near 68°F and lows around 46°F, though wet days run closer to 11.
usfs:tn-cherokee-national-forest.whereToBase: Hot Springs sits just 2 miles from the boundary, making it the closest jumping-off point. Parrottsville is a touch farther at 2.6 miles, while Newport lies about 3.6 miles out—all outside the forest but within easy reach of the trailheads.

usfs:tx-angelina-national-forest.whyCome: One of four national forests in the piney woods region of East Texas, Angelina spans parts of San Augustine, Angelina, Jasper and Nacogdoches counties. Set in the Neches River Basin, it wraps the north and south shores of Sam Rayburn Reservoir. Longleaf pine dominates the south, while loblolly and shortleaf pine fill the northern reaches and grow abundantly throughout.
usfs:tx-angelina-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest surrounds Sam Rayburn Reservoir, a man-made lake covering 452.3 square kilometers formed by the Sam Rayburn Dam on the Angelina River in 1965, with three campgrounds and facilities along its shores. Smaller Lake Kurth adds another camping spot. Two more national forests lie close by: Sabine, roughly 4 miles away, and Davy Crockett, about 11 miles off.
usfs:tx-angelina-national-forest.whenToGo: For milder days, autumn typically shines: October averages highs near 81°F with lows around 64°F and just 6 wet days. Winter stays temperate, with January highs near 58°F and lows around 42°F. Summers run hot, with July and August highs in the low 90s.

usfs:tx-davy-crockett-national-forest.whyCome: Davy Crockett National Forest spreads across 389,609 acres of southeast Texas, tucked between the towns of Lufkin and Crockett. Administered by the U.S. Forest Service, with district offices in Ratcliff, it offers a quiet expanse of piney woods well away from the crowds of the state's better-known destinations.
usfs:tx-davy-crockett-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits within a broader cluster of East Texas public land: Angelina National Forest lies about 11 miles off, and the larger Sam Houston National Forest roughly 22 miles away, together giving the region hundreds of thousands of additional acres to explore. Nearby Lake Livingston, a 336.7-square-kilometer reservoir formed by the Livingston Dam on the Trinity River in 1969, adds open water to the mix.
usfs:tx-davy-crockett-national-forest.whenToGo: Fall is typically the driest and mildest stretch, with September and October highs around 91°F and 82°F and just 6 to 7 wet days per month. For cooler air, winter months like January bring highs near 58°F, though rain picks up to about 10 days.

usfs:tx-sabine-national-forest.whyCome: Set in East Texas along the Texas-Louisiana border, Sabine National Forest spreads across 454,542 acres of pine woods and reservoir shoreline. Administered from common offices in Lufkin with a local ranger district in Hemphill, it anchors the easternmost of Texas's national forests, drawing anglers, boaters, and campers to a quiet corner of the state.
usfs:tx-sabine-national-forest.dontMiss: Two large man-made reservoirs shape the forest. Toledo Bend Reservoir spans 670.2 square kilometers, impounded by the Toledo Bend Dam on the Sabine River (completed 1966). Nearby Sam Rayburn Reservoir covers 452.3 square kilometers behind Sam Rayburn Dam (1965), with three campgrounds and facilities. Angelina National Forest lies about 4 miles away, and Louisiana's vast Kisatchie National Forest is roughly 19 miles off.
usfs:tx-sabine-national-forest.whenToGo: Autumn is typically the driest stretch: October sees highs near 81°F, lows around 62°F, and only about 5 wet days. Spring runs mild but wetter, with April highs around 77°F and roughly 12 wet days. Summers turn hot, with August highs near 95°F.

usfs:tx-sam-houston-national-forest.whyCome: One of four national forests in Texas, Sam Houston sits just 50 miles north of Houston, spread across Montgomery, San Jacinto, and Walker counties. Covering 495,316 acres, it offers an accessible stretch of East Texas woodland administered alongside its sister forests and grasslands, with a local ranger district office in New Waverly.
usfs:tx-sam-houston-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest anchors two sizable man-made reservoirs: Lake Livingston, at 336.7 square kilometers, dammed on the Trinity River in 1969, and Lake Conroe, covering 80.1 square kilometers. Davy Crockett National Forest lies about 22 miles away, its 389,609 acres extending the region's public woodland well beyond this forest's own bounds.
usfs:tx-sam-houston-national-forest.whenToGo: Fall is typically the driest stretch, with October highs near 82°F and just 7 wet days, easing into November's 70°F. Winter stays mild, with January highs around 59°F. Summers run hot and humid, typically climbing to 95°F in August.

usfs:ut-ashley-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning more than 1.4 million acres across northeastern Utah and southwestern Wyoming, Ashley National Forest holds vast forests, lakes, and mountains, with elevations from 6,000 to 13,500 feet. Its best-known landmarks include the Flaming Gorge National Recreation Area and the Uinta Mountains, home to Utah's highest peak. The forest also contains about 60.5% of the High Uintas Wilderness.
usfs:ut-ashley-national-forest.dontMiss: Ashley shares a boundary with the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest, extending your range across unbroken public land, with the Manti-La Sal National Forest 13 miles off. The 157.4-km2 Flaming Gorge Reservoir, formed by the 1964 Flaming Gorge Dam on the Green River, anchors the water recreation, while Strawberry Reservoir adds five campgrounds and facilities across its 65.6 km2.
usfs:ut-ashley-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the mildest window: July typically sees highs of 65°F and lows of 42°F, though rain falls on about 16 days. September cools to highs near 55°F with lows of 33°F and around 12 wet days, a quieter shoulder option.
usfs:ut-ashley-national-forest.whereToBase: Dutch John sits inside the forest at the boundary. Manila lies just 0.3 miles out, with Vernal—home to the forest headquarters—0.7 miles from the edge and Duchesne 0.9 miles away, all convenient jumping-off points.

usfs:ut-dixie-national-forest.whyCome: Stretching about 170 miles across southern Utah, Dixie is the largest national forest in the state, covering nearly two million acres. Headquartered in Cedar City, it straddles the divide between the Great Basin and the Colorado River, spilling across six counties with the majority of its acreage in Garfield County. It's high country between some of the West's most storied desert landscapes.
usfs:ut-dixie-national-forest.dontMiss: Dixie's edges put you next to remarkable public land: Bryce Canyon National Park shares its boundary, and the forest lies just 3 miles from Zion and 9 miles from Capitol Reef. It also adjoins Fishlake National Forest, an even larger neighbor. Water-based options include Otter Creek Reservoir, with three campgrounds and a recreation area, and vast Lake Powell, held back by Glen Canyon Dam on the Colorado River since 1963.
usfs:ut-dixie-national-forest.whenToGo: For warm-weather trips, June through September typically brings highs from 65 to 74°F, though July and August see the most rain, with 14 and 13 wet days. Autumn is drier: October typically reaches 53°F with just 5 wet days, and November averages 41°F with 4.
usfs:ut-dixie-national-forest.whereToBase: Several towns sit right inside the forest, including Alton, Boulder, and the mountain community of Brian Head, all at the boundary. Just outside, Bryce Canyon City lies at the edge, and Panguitch is only half a mile from the forest boundary, making a convenient gateway.

usfs:ut-fishlake-national-forest.whyCome: Spread across south central Utah, Fishlake National Forest covers roughly 1,787,975 acres of high country. It takes its name from Fish Lake, the largest freshwater mountain lake in the state, and the wider landscape strings together forested ridgelines, natural lakes, and reservoirs across a broad slice of the region.
usfs:ut-fishlake-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits directly against Capitol Reef National Park and two neighboring national forests, Manti-La Sal and Dixie, opening onto millions of adjoining acres of public land. Fish Lake itself, the state's largest freshwater mountain lake at 10.4 square kilometers, anchors seven campgrounds and facilities. Nearby Otter Creek Reservoir adds three more and a recreation area.
usfs:ut-fishlake-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the most settled stretch: July typically sees highs near 85°F, lows around 61°F, and just 2 wet days, with June close behind at 79°F and 3 wet days. Early fall stays comfortable, with September highs near 75°F and only 3 wet days.
usfs:ut-fishlake-national-forest.whereToBase: All of the nearest towns sit just outside the boundary. Beaver and Fillmore both lie about 0.2 miles out, with Torrey roughly 0.3 miles from the edge near the Capitol Reef side. Loa (0.5 miles) and Monroe (0.8 miles) offer additional jumping-off points.

usfs:ut-manti-la-sal-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning more than 1.2 million acres across central and southeastern Utah and into western Colorado, Manti–La Sal National Forest climbs to 12,721 feet at Mount Peale, the crown of the La Sal Mountains—Utah's second highest range after the Uintas. Headquartered in Price, with parts folded into Bears Ears National Monument, it pairs high peaks with broad canyon country.
usfs:ut-manti-la-sal-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits close to two national parks—Arches just 2 miles off and Canyonlands within 3—while sharing an unbroken boundary with the Uinta-Wasatch-Cache and Fishlake National Forests. Among its waters, Scofield Reservoir on the Price River (dammed 1946) offers a named recreation area with two rec areas and a campground, and natural Fish Lake anchors seven campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:ut-manti-la-sal-national-forest.whenToGo: Late spring is typically dry and mild, with May highs near 79°F, lows around 54°F, and essentially no wet days. Fall brings cooler air—October highs near 67°F and lows around 47°F—with roughly four wet days. High summer runs hot, with July highs typically near 98°F.
usfs:ut-manti-la-sal-national-forest.whereToBase: Several towns sit just outside the boundary. Ferron is closest at 0.2 miles, followed by Wales at 0.6 miles. Ephraim and Price both lie about 0.8 miles out, and Manti about 1.1 miles from the forest edge.

usfs:ut-uinta-wasatch-cache-national-for.whyCome: Spread across northern Utah with smaller reaches into southeastern Idaho and southwestern Wyoming, Uinta-Wasatch-Cache National Forest takes its name from a Ute word for a low place in high mountains and the French cache, once used by fur trappers. Its boundaries enclose more than 1.6 million acres of mountain country carved by lakes, rivers, and forested ranges.
usfs:ut-uinta-wasatch-cache-national-for.dontMiss: The forest shares borders with Manti-La Sal, Caribou-Targhee, and Ashley National Forests, opening a broad connected landscape to explore. Great Salt Lake, at 3,389.5 square kilometers, and natural Bear Lake anchor the region, while man-made Strawberry Reservoir, backed by the 1974 Soldier Creek Dam, offers five campgrounds and facilities for those planning to linger by the water.
usfs:ut-uinta-wasatch-cache-national-for.whenToGo: Summer is the reliably dry window: July typically sees highs near 84°F, lows around 54°F, and just 4 wet days, with August and September close behind. For cooler, snowier conditions, January and February typically bring highs in the low 30s and roughly 10 wet days each.
usfs:ut-uinta-wasatch-cache-national-for.whereToBase: Independence, Brighton, and Alta all sit inside the forest boundary, placing you within the mountains themselves. If you prefer to stay just outside, Pleasant Grove lies about 0.3 miles from the boundary and Kamas about 0.5 miles away, both convenient jumping-off points for day trips.

usfs:va-george-washington-and-jefferson-.whyCome: One of the largest areas of public land in the Eastern United States, this Appalachian expanse spreads across Virginia, West Virginia, and Kentucky. Roughly 1 million of its acres remain remote and undeveloped, and 139,461 acres are designated wilderness where future development is prohibited. It's a landscape built for solitude, ridgelines, and mountain quiet.
usfs:va-george-washington-and-jefferson-.dontMiss: The forest shares its boundary with Cherokee and Monongahela National Forests and sits just a mile from Shenandoah National Park, stitching together an immense corridor of public land. Anglers and boaters can point toward its flagship reservoirs, including the 174-square-kilometer John H. Kerr Reservoir, dammed on the Roanoke River in 1953 and served by three campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:va-george-washington-and-jefferson-.whenToGo: Early fall is typically the driest stretch, with September averaging highs near 73°F and about 8 wet days, followed by October highs around 63°F. Spring shoulders warm gradually, with April highs near 62°F, though May typically sees around 16 wet days.
usfs:va-george-washington-and-jefferson-.whereToBase: Goshen, New Castle, and Clifton Forge all sit inside the forest boundary, making them natural launch points for the surrounding ridges. Iron Gate lies within it as well, while Craigsville rests just 0.2 miles outside.

usfs:vt-green-mountain-and-finger-lakes-.whyCome: Spread across 854,081 acres of Vermont, Green Mountain and Finger Lakes National Forests gather the wooded ridgelines and quiet water that define the state's interior. The scale alone is the draw: a broad public landscape stitched together for hikers, paddlers, and anyone content to let a mountain forest set the pace.
usfs:vt-green-mountain-and-finger-lakes-.dontMiss: The forest sits near the 946,821-acre White Mountain National Forest, roughly two miles away across the New Hampshire line, putting a second vast block of public land within easy reach for a longer trip. On the water, Lake Champlain stretches across 1,121.5 square kilometers, one of the region's great lakes for boating and open-water views.
usfs:vt-green-mountain-and-finger-lakes-.whenToGo: Early fall is comfortable and among the drier stretches: September typically brings highs near 73°F with about 10 wet days, easing into October's 63°F and roughly 8 wet days. For summer warmth, July peaks around 82°F, though expect close to 14 wet days.
usfs:vt-green-mountain-and-finger-lakes-.whereToBase: Manchester, North Bennington, and Old Bennington all sit inside the forest boundary, making them natural starting points close to trailheads and water. Just outside, Woodstock lies about 3.2 miles from the boundary and Hoosick Falls about 3.8 miles, both within a short drive of the forest edge.

usfs:wa-colville-national-forest.whyCome: Colville National Forest spreads across 1,752,995 acres of northeastern Washington, tucked into the state's forested corner near the Canadian border. It is bordered on the west by the Okanogan–Wenatchee National Forest and on the east by the Idaho Panhandle. The result is a vast, quiet expanse of woods, water, and mountain country far from crowds.
usfs:wa-colville-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest lies within reach of two neighboring giants: the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, at 3,858,129 acres, and the Idaho Panhandle National Forests, at 2,943,333 acres, both meeting Colville directly. Nearby waters include the man-made Lake Pend Oreille, at 375.4 square kilometers behind Albeni Falls Dam, and Franklin D. Roosevelt Lake, at 288.6 square kilometers, held by the 1941 Grand Coulee Dam.
usfs:wa-colville-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the clear window: July typically sees highs near 76°F with just 4 wet days, and September stays mild around 65°F with about 6 wet days. Winters run cold and damp, with January highs near 28°F and December averaging 15 wet days.
usfs:wa-colville-national-forest.whereToBase: Metaline Falls sits right at the boundary, while Republic and Kettle Falls lie just outside, roughly 0.1 and 0.3 miles from the forest edge. Tonasket, about 0.3 miles out, offers another approach point to the western reaches.

usfs:wa-gifford-pinchot-national-forest.whyCome: Stretching across 1.32 million acres of southern Washington's Cascade Range, Gifford Pinchot National Forest reaches 72 miles from Mount Rainier National Park to the Columbia River. Broad old-growth forests, high mountain meadows, glaciers, and volcanic peaks define it, crowned by 12,276-foot Mount Adams, the state's second-tallest volcano. Within its bounds lies the 110,000-acre Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument, established by Congress in 1982.
usfs:wa-gifford-pinchot-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest sits amid a vast public-land network: it borders Mount Rainier National Park, meets Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest, and folds into Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest, with Mt. Hood National Forest just five miles off. Along the Columbia River, Lake Bonneville spreads across 78.7 square kilometers behind the 1938 Bonneville Locks and Dam, offering a campground and recreation area.
usfs:wa-gifford-pinchot-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 76°F and only about 2 wet days, with August close behind at 75°F and 4 wet days. Winters run wet and cool, December averaging highs of 36°F and roughly 21 wet days, so plan accordingly.
usfs:wa-gifford-pinchot-national-forest.whereToBase: All bases sit just outside the boundary. Morton lies closest at about 1.5 miles out, a handy northern gateway. To the south along the Columbia, Stevenson (3.3 miles) and White Salmon (3.9 miles) put you near the forest's river edge and Mount Adams country.

usfs:wa-mt-baker-snoqualmie-national-for.whyCome: Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forest stretches more than 140 miles along the western slopes of the Cascade Range, running from the Canada–US border to the northern edge of Mount Rainier National Park. Spanning over two million acres, it offers a vast expanse of Cascade forest and mountain terrain administered by the U.S. Forest Service, with ranger districts scattered from Sedro-Woolley to Enumclaw.
usfs:wa-mt-baker-snoqualmie-national-for.dontMiss: The forest neighbors an extraordinary run of public land: it borders both Mount Rainier and North Cascades National Parks, adjoins Gifford Pinchot National Forest, and lies within the sprawling 3,858,129-acre Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest. Baker Lake, a 19.1-square-kilometer reservoir formed by the 1959 Upper Baker dam on the Baker River, anchors the recreation side with ten campgrounds and facilities.
usfs:wa-mt-baker-snoqualmie-national-for.whenToGo: Summer is the driest window: July typically sees highs of 67°F, lows near 47°F, and just 4 wet days. August stays warm at 67°F but climbs to 10 wet days. Winter is soaking—December typically brings highs of 32°F and around 22 wet days.
usfs:wa-mt-baker-snoqualmie-national-for.whereToBase: North Bend sits inside the forest, right at the boundary. Skykomish lies just 0.1 miles out, and Darrington is about 0.2 miles from the edge—both convenient jumping-off points along different sections of this long, ranger-district-lined forest.

usfs:wa-okanogan-wenatchee-national-fore.whyCome: Spread across roughly 3.9 million acres of north-central Washington, the Okanogan-Wenatchee National Forest anchors Okanogan County's mountain country. It stretches through the Cascades, meeting some of the state's most celebrated high-country landscapes and offering the deep-forest scale that defines Washington's interior ranges.
usfs:wa-okanogan-wenatchee-national-fore.dontMiss: The forest borders both Mount Rainier National Park and North Cascades National Park, and connects with the Colville, Gifford Pinchot, and Mt. Baker-Snoqualmie National Forests, making for vast contiguous public land. Lake Chelan, its flagship at 132.2 square kilometers, was formed by the 1927 Chelan Dam and offers a campground and a recreation area on its shores.
usfs:wa-okanogan-wenatchee-national-fore.whenToGo: For the driest, warmest stretch, July and August typically bring highs of 83 and 82 with just 1 to 2 wet days per month. Late spring is milder: May typically reaches 62 with around 6 wet days, a good balance before summer heat settles in.
usfs:wa-okanogan-wenatchee-national-fore.whereToBase: Roslyn sits inside the forest itself. Twisp lies about 0.1 miles from the boundary, while Cle Elum and Leavenworth are each roughly 0.2 miles out. Entiat, about 0.3 miles from the edge, rounds out the nearby options for reaching the forest's varied terrain.

usfs:wa-olympic-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning 697,411 acres across Washington's Olympic Peninsula, this national forest nearly surrounds Olympic National Park and the Olympic Mountain range. Its landscape shifts dramatically, from the temperate Olympic rain forest to the salt water fjord of Hood Canal to the peaks of Mt. Washington, gathering some of the state's most varied terrain into one sprawling public expanse.
usfs:wa-olympic-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest wraps around the 913,603-acre Olympic National Park, so trips here pair easily with the park itself. Water is central: natural Lake Crescent and Lake Quinault, the latter ringed by seven campgrounds and facilities, sit alongside man-made Lake Cushman, held by the 1926 Cushman No 1 dam, and Wynoochee Lake, backed by its 1972 dam.
usfs:wa-olympic-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the driest stretch: July typically sees highs near 79°F, lows around 54°F, and just 3 wet days, with August similar at 79°F and 6 wet days. Winters are wet and mild, with December typically bringing 23 wet days and highs of 46°F.
usfs:wa-olympic-national-forest.whereToBase: All these towns sit just outside the boundary. Forks is closest at about 2.7 miles, followed by Tumwater around 3.2 miles. Port Angeles lies roughly 4.8 miles out, offering a northern gateway toward the peninsula's coast and mountains.

usfs:wi-chequamegon-nicolet-national-for.whyCome: Spanning more than 1,530,647 acres across northern Wisconsin, the Chequamegon–Nicolet National Forest is legally two forests—Chequamegon and Nicolet—managed as one unit since 1998. Both were established by presidential proclamation in 1933. Their woods belong to the North Woods ecoregion of the upper Great Lakes, with second-growth stands, some planted by the Civilian Conservation Corps in the 1930s.
usfs:wi-chequamegon-nicolet-national-for.dontMiss: The forest runs right up against the 1,562,423-acre Ottawa National Forest, so a visit here opens directly onto adjoining public land and a vast continuous stretch of North Woods country. Water is everywhere: Lake Chippewa, the Turtle-Flambeau Flowage, and Lake Gogebic, held back by the 1906 Bergland Dam, are among the notable lakes within reach.
usfs:wi-chequamegon-nicolet-national-for.whenToGo: Summer is the mildest stretch: July typically sees highs near 77°F and lows around 60°F, with about 12 wet days. September stays pleasant, typically reaching 70°F with lows near 54°F and fewer wet days, around 9. Winters run cold, with January highs of just 22°F.
usfs:wi-chequamegon-nicolet-national-for.whereToBase: All the gateway towns sit just outside the boundary. Medford is closest at 0.3 miles, followed by Eagle River at 0.5 miles and Hayward at 0.6 miles. Rhinelander (0.9 miles) and Park Falls (1 mile) round out the options for easy access to the forest.

usfs:wv-monongahela-national-forest.whyCome: Set in the Allegheny Mountains of eastern West Virginia, Monongahela National Forest protects over 921,000 acres of federally managed land within a 1,700,000-acre proclamation boundary. It spans much of the Potomac Highlands Region and reaches into portions of 10 counties, offering a vast, forested landscape across the state's high country.
usfs:wv-monongahela-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest borders the George Washington and Jefferson National Forests, extending public land seamlessly across nearly 3.5 million acres, while New River Gorge National Park sits about 19 miles away. Water anchors much of the recreation: Lake Moomaw, behind Gathright Dam on the Jackson River, offers six campgrounds and facilities plus a rec area, and Summersville Lake fills behind a 1965 dam on the Gauley River.
usfs:wv-monongahela-national-forest.whenToGo: Early fall is a comfortable window: September typically sees highs near 65°F with about 11 wet days, and October cools to the mid-50s with roughly 9 wet days, the driest stretch of the year. Winter runs cold, with January highs around 30°F and 17 wet days.
usfs:wv-monongahela-national-forest.whereToBase: All five basecamps sit inside the forest boundary. Marlinton, Huttonsville, and Richwood each lie at the boundary, giving direct access to the surrounding high country. Parsons and Hambleton round out the options for travelers wanting to stay close to the forest.

usfs:wy-bighorn-national-forest.whyCome: One of the oldest government-protected forest lands in the U.S., established as a Forest Reserve in 1897, Bighorn National Forest spans over 1.1 million acres in northern Wyoming. Set well east of the continental divide, it follows the spine of the Bighorn Mountains for 80 miles, from sagebrush lowlands at 5,000 feet to 13,189-foot Cloud Peak, the range's highest point.
usfs:wy-bighorn-national-forest.dontMiss: The Bighorns stand apart as an outlying range, separated from the rest of the Rocky Mountains by the Bighorn Basin, giving the forest a distinct island character. Roughly 99% of its land sits above 4,900 feet, so high country dominates. To the north, near the Montana border, the vast Custer Gallatin National Forest lies about 19 miles away, adding over 3.4 million acres of nearby public land.
usfs:wy-bighorn-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs of 64°F with lows around 42°F, while August warms similarly at 61°F and brings the fewest wet days, around 11. September stays pleasant, typically 55°F, with only about 8 wet days—the driest stretch of the year.
usfs:wy-bighorn-national-forest.whereToBase: Buffalo sits closest, just 0.4 miles from the boundary, followed by Greybull at 1 mile and Sheridan at 1.8 miles. Dayton (3.4 miles) and Ten Sleep (5.9 miles) offer additional gateways spread around the range.

usfs:wy-bridger-teton-national-forest.whyCome: Spanning 3.4 million acres of western Wyoming, Bridger-Teton is the third largest National Forest outside Alaska. It stretches from Yellowstone along the eastern edge of Grand Teton, then rides the western slope of the Continental Divide to the Wind River Range, reaching south to the Salt River and Wyoming ranges near the Idaho border.
usfs:wy-bridger-teton-national-forest.dontMiss: The forest shares borders with Grand Teton and Yellowstone National Parks, and abuts the Caribou-Targhee and Shoshone National Forests, placing it at the heart of a vast connected wildland. Within reach are Jackson Lake, with five campgrounds and facilities, and Palisades Reservoir, formed by the 1957 Palisades Dam on the South Fork Snake River.
usfs:wy-bridger-teton-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically sees highs near 75°F, lows around 47°F, and just 7 wet days, with August close behind at 71°F and 9 wet days. September stays pleasant, typically topping out around 64°F with 8 wet days. Winters are cold and snowy.
usfs:wy-bridger-teton-national-forest.whereToBase: Alpine sits closest, about 0.1 miles from the boundary, with Afton and Star Valley Ranch each roughly 0.2 miles out. For the Wind River side, Pinedale and Big Piney both lie about 0.3 miles from the forest edge.

usfs:wy-medicine-bow-routt-national-fore.whyCome: Spanning more than 4.5 million acres across Wyoming and Colorado, Medicine Bow-Routt National Forest brings together what were once three separate areas: Medicine Bow National Forest, Routt National Forest, and Thunder Basin National Grassland, administratively combined in 1995 for their shared resources and proximity. It's a broad, high-country expanse where mountain forest and grassland meet.
usfs:wy-medicine-bow-routt-national-fore.dontMiss: The forest borders Rocky Mountain National Park and the Arapaho and Roosevelt and White River National Forests, opening a vast continuous stretch of public land. Lake Granby, a 28.6-square-kilometer reservoir formed by Granby Dam on the Colorado River (1950), stands out for recreation, with eight campgrounds and facilities and a named recreation area. Nearby Glendo Reservoir also offers a named recreation area.
usfs:wy-medicine-bow-routt-national-fore.whenToGo: Summer is the mildest and driest window: July and August typically see highs of 76°F and 74°F with lows in the low 50s and just six wet days each month. September stays pleasant, with highs around 66°F and six wet days. Winter runs cold, with January highs near 19°F.
usfs:wy-medicine-bow-routt-national-fore.whereToBase: Steamboat Springs sits just 0.2 miles from the boundary and offers the most services, with 29 lodgings and 39 places to eat, plus ski and brewery draws. Yampa lies 0.2 miles out and Kremmling 0.5 miles from the boundary, both handy alternatives close to the forest edge.

usfs:wy-shoshone-national-forest.whyCome: Established in 1891, Shoshone National Forest is the first federally protected National Forest in the United States, spanning nearly 2,500,000 acres of Wyoming. Native Americans have lived in the region for at least 10,000 years, and the forest — part of the Greater Yellowstone Ecosystem — has retained most of its wildness, never heavily settled or exploited.
usfs:wy-shoshone-national-forest.dontMiss: Shoshone forms part of a nearly unbroken expanse of protected land: it borders Yellowstone National Park and Bridger-Teton National Forest directly, with Custer Gallatin National Forest and Grand Teton within reach. Buffalo Bill Reservoir, held by the 1910 Buffalo Bill Dam on the Shoshone River, anchors the recreation, with four rec areas and a campground alongside its 26.4-square-kilometer waters.
usfs:wy-shoshone-national-forest.whenToGo: Summer is the reliable window: July typically reaches highs near 71°F with lows around 47°F and about 9 wet days. September stays mild, typically peaking near 61°F with lows near 38°F and the driest stretch at roughly 7 wet days. Winters run cold, with January highs of just 23°F.
usfs:wy-shoshone-national-forest.whereToBase: Dubois sits closest to the boundary at about 0.4 miles, with Cody just beyond at 0.5 miles and Lander at 1.4 miles. All three lie outside the forest, making natural staging points for exploring the surrounding country.
